topics.title: Basic Concepts - Dirac Notations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET863 - QUANTUM COMPUTING WITH PYTHON
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Concepts - Dirac Notations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Dirac Notation

**Dirac Notation** (also called **Bra-Ket notation** or **Bracket notation**) is a standardized symbolic framework introduced by physicist **Paul Adrien Maurice Dirac** in **1939** to describe quantum states, operators, and inner products in a compact, linear-algebraic manner. It is the de facto language of quantum mechanics and, by extension, quantum computing.

In KTU's 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Quantum Computing with Python*, Dirac notation forms the **foundational mathematical vocabulary** upon which all quantum gates, qubits, superposition, entanglement, and measurement operators are expressed. The notation is mandatory for writing quantum circuits in frameworks such as Qiskit, Cirq, and PennyLane.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Every quantum state in this course will be written using Dirac notation. Whether the implementation is in Python (NumPy/Qiskit) or mathematical reasoning, the Dirac bracket system is the universal translator between abstract quantum theory and executable code.

## 1.2 The Two Fundamental Components

The Dirac notation has two primitive building blocks:

### (a) The Ket — $|\psi\rangle$

A **Ket** denotes a **column vector** representing a quantum state. The symbol $\psi$ (psi) is a label for the state, while the enclosing $| \cdot \rangle$ indicates that this is a state vector in a complex Hilbert space $\mathcal{H}$.

$$\ket{\psi} = \begin{pmatrix} \alpha_1 \\ \alpha_2 \\ \vdots \\ \alpha_n \end{pmatrix}, \quad \alpha_i \in \mathbb{C}$$

The set $\{\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \ldots, \alpha_n\}$ are called the **probability amplitudes**. The squared moduli $|\alpha_i|^2$ yield the measurement probabilities.

### (b) The Bra — $\langle\psi|$

A **Bra** denotes a **row vector** — the **complex conjugate transpose** (Hermitian adjoint) of the corresponding ket. It lives in the **dual Hilbert space** $\mathcal{H}^*$.

$$\bra{\psi} = \ket{\psi}^\dagger = (\alpha_1^*, \alpha_2^*, \ldots, \alpha_n^*)$$

The combination of a bra and a ket forms a **bracket** — hence the name *Bra-Ket* notation.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Voting Ballot Box" Picture**
>
> Imagine a glass ballot box representing a quantum system:
>
> - The **Ket $|\psi\rangle$** is the box itself with all the ballots (amplitudes) stacked vertically inside. It is the *physical state* of the system.
> - The **Bra $\langle\psi|$** is the *mirror image* placed beside the box, showing each ballot flipped upside-down and reflected. It is the *mathematical conjugate* used to "extract" information from the box.
> - The **Bracket $\langle\phi|\psi\rangle$** (an inner product) is the act of comparing the mirror of one box against another box — yielding a single number (the *overlap*).
> - The **Ket-Bra $|\psi\rangle\langle\phi|$** is the act of stacking one box against another's mirror, producing an *operator* that can act on any other box.
>
> Just as you cannot meaningfully "add" two ballot boxes without first aligning them, you cannot combine kets and bras without respecting their bra-ket conjugation rules.

## 1.4 Computational Basis States (Special Kets)

The most elementary kets are the **computational basis states** of a single qubit:

$$\ket{0} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \ket{1} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

Their corresponding bras are:

$$\bra{0} = (1, 0), \quad \bra{1} = (0, 1)$$

These are the **canonical orthonormal basis** used by quantum hardware (superconducting qubits, trapped ions, photon polarization).

## 1.5 Why Dirac Notation Matters in KTU's Course

| Aspect | Benefit |
|---|---|
| **Compactness** | A column of $2^n$ complex numbers is written as a single symbol. |
| **Physical clarity** | Separates "state" (ket) from "measurement observable" (operator). |
| **Language-agnostic** | Same notation in math, Python (Qiskit), and textbooks. |
| **Tensor-friendly** | Multi-qubit systems are built using $\otimes$ elegantly. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric to Remember:**
> For an $n$-qubit system, the Hilbert space dimension is $d = 2^n$, and the state is described by $2^n$ complex amplitudes. For $n=1$, $d=2$; for $n=2$, $d=4$; for $n=10$, $d=1024$ (the regime where quantum advantage emerges).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algebraic Components of Dirac Notation

Dirac notation operates on three structural levels:

1. **Vectors (kets / bras)** — describe states.
2. **Scalars (brackets $\langle\phi|\psi\rangle$)** — describe overlaps / inner products.
3. **Operators (ket-bras $|\psi\rangle\langle\phi|$)** — describe transformations.

### 2.1.1 Inner Product (Bracket) — $\langle\phi|\psi\rangle$

The inner product of two kets $|\psi\rangle$ and $|\phi\rangle$ is the **scalar** formed by multiplying the bra $\langle\phi|$ (row) with the ket $|\psi\rangle$ (column):

$$\langle\phi|\psi\rangle = (\beta_1^*, \beta_2^*, \ldots, \beta_n^*)\begin{pmatrix} \alpha_1 \\ \alpha_2 \\ \vdots \\ \alpha_n \end{pmatrix} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \beta_i^* \alpha_i$$

**Properties:**
- $\langle\psi|\psi\rangle \geq 0$ (positive semi-definite).
- $\langle\phi|\psi\rangle = \langle\psi|\phi\rangle^*$ (conjugate symmetry).
- $\langle\psi|\psi\rangle = 1$ for **normalized states** (probability conservation).

### 2.1.2 Outer Product (Ket-Bra) — $|\psi\rangle\langle\phi|$

The outer product of a ket and a bra is an **operator** (matrix):

$$|\psi\rangle\langle\phi| = \begin{pmatrix} \alpha_1 \\ \alpha_2 \\ \vdots \\ \alpha_n \end{pmatrix}(\beta_1^*, \beta_2^*, \ldots, \beta_n^*) = \begin{pmatrix} \alpha_1\beta_1^* & \alpha_1\beta_2^* & \cdots \\ \alpha_2\beta_1^* & \alpha_2\beta_2^* & \cdots \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots \end{pmatrix}$$

**Identity operator** in an $n$-dimensional space:

$$I = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \ket{i}\bra{i} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \ket{e_i}\bra{e_i}$$

This is the **completeness relation** (or *resolution of identity*) — one of the most powerful identities in quantum mechanics.

### 2.1.3 Tensor Product (Multi-Qubit States) — $|\psi\rangle \otimes |\phi\rangle$

For a two-qubit system, the joint state lives in $\mathcal{H}_1 \otimes \mathcal{H}_2$:

$$\ket{\psi} \otimes \ket{\phi} \equiv \ket{\psi\phi} \equiv \ket{\psi}\ket{\phi}$$

For basis states:

$$\ket{0}\otimes\ket{0} = \ket{00} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \ket{0}\otimes\ket{1} = \ket{01} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

## 2.2 Operator Notation in Dirac Form

Any linear operator $\hat{A}$ acting on a ket $|\psi\rangle$ can be written as:

$$\hat{A}\ket{\psi} = \ket{\psi'}$$

The **matrix elements** of $\hat{A}$ in some basis $\{|i\rangle\}$ are:

$$A_{ij} = \bra{i}\hat{A}\ket{j}$$

The **Hermitian adjoint** of $\hat{A}$ is denoted $\hat{A}^\dagger$:

$$\bra{\psi}\hat{A}^\dagger = (\hat{A}\ket{\psi})^\dagger, \quad \bra{\phi}\hat{A}\ket{\psi} = (\bra{\psi}\hat{A}^\dagger\ket{\phi})^*$$

## 2.3 Eigenvalue Equations in Dirac Form

If $|\psi\rangle$ is an eigenvector of operator $\hat{A}$ with eigenvalue $\lambda$:

$$\hat{A}\ket{\psi} = \lambda\ket{\psi}$$

For a Hermitian operator, $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$, and eigenvectors of distinct eigenvalues are orthogonal.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Name | Meaning | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\ket{\psi}$ | Ket | Column vector state | Vector in $\mathcal{H}$ |
| $\bra{\psi}$ | Bra | Row vector (dual) | Vector in $\mathcal{H}^*$ |
| $\langle\phi \mid \psi\rangle$ | Inner product / Bracket | $\sum_i \beta_i^* \alpha_i$ | Scalar (complex) |
| $\ket{\phi}\bra{\psi}$ | Outer product | Matrix | Operator |
| $\hat{A}^\dagger$ | Hermitian adjoint | Conjugate transpose | Operator |
| $\ket{\psi}^*$ | Complex conjugate | Component-wise conjugate | — |
| $\bra{\psi}\hat{A}\ket{\phi}$ | Sandwich / Matrix element | $A_{\psi\phi}$ | Scalar |
| $\hat{A}\ket{\psi} = \lambda\ket{\psi}$ | Eigen-equation | $\hat{A}$ acts on $\ket{\psi}$ | Eigenvector eqn. |
| $I = \sum_i \ket{i}\bra{i}$ | Completeness relation | Identity on $\mathcal{H}$ | Operator |
| $\ket{ab} = \ket{a}\otimes\ket{b}$ | Tensor product | Joint qubit state | Vector in $\mathcal{H}^{\otimes n}$ |
| $\hat{U}^\dagger \hat{U} = I$ | Unitarity | Reversible evolution | Operator |
| $[\hat{A}, \hat{B}] = \hat{A}\hat{B} - \hat{B}\hat{A}$ | Commutator | Measures non-commutativity | Operator |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> In quantum computing, every quantum gate (Hadamard $H$, Pauli $X$, CNOT, Toffoli) is expressed as an operator in Dirac notation. The state-vector output of a Qiskit simulation is, internally, the coefficient array of a ket $|\psi\rangle$. Without Dirac notation, reasoning about multi-qubit entanglement and measurement would be impractical.

## 2.5 Norm and Normalization

A state $|\psi\rangle$ is **normalized** if and only if:

$$\langle\psi|\psi\rangle = 1$$

If $\langle\psi|\psi\rangle = N$, the normalized state is:

$$\ket{\psi_{\text{norm}}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}}\ket{\psi}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Probability Interpretation (Born Rule):**
> If a system is in state $|\psi\rangle = \sum_i \alpha_i |i\rangle$, the probability of obtaining outcome $i$ upon measurement in the basis $\{|i\rangle\}$ is:
> $$P(i) = \vert\langle i \mid \psi\rangle\vert^2 = \vert\alpha_i\vert^2$$
> This is the cornerstone of measurement in quantum computing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Inner Product of Two Arbitrary Qubit States

**Problem:** Compute $\langle\phi|\psi\rangle$ for two single-qubit states:

$$\ket{\psi} = \alpha\ket{0} + \beta\ket{1}, \quad \ket{\phi} = \gamma\ket{0} + \delta\ket{1}$$

where $\alpha, \beta, \gamma, \delta \in \mathbb{C}$.

**Step 1 — Express both states as column vectors** in the computational basis $\{|0\rangle, |1\rangle\}$:

$$\ket{\psi} = \begin{pmatrix} \alpha \\ \beta \end{pmatrix}, \quad \ket{\phi} = \begin{pmatrix} \gamma \\ \delta \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Form the bra $\langle\phi|$** by taking the conjugate transpose of $|\phi\rangle$:

$$\bra{\phi} = \begin{pmatrix} \gamma^* & \delta^* \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply bra $\times$ ket** (row $\times$ column = scalar):

$$\langle\phi|\psi\rangle = \begin{pmatrix} \gamma^* & \delta^* \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} \alpha \\ \beta \end{pmatrix} = \gamma^*\alpha + \delta^*\beta$$

**Step 4 — Final symbolic form:**

$$\boxed{\langle\phi|\psi\rangle = \gamma^*\alpha + \delta^*\beta}$$

> This is a complex scalar; the modulus squared $|\langle\phi|\psi\rangle|^2$ gives the **fidelity** or **transition probability** between the two states.

## 3.2 Derivation: Outer Product $|\psi\rangle\langle\phi|$ as a Matrix

**Problem:** Compute $|\psi\rangle\langle\phi|$ for the same two qubits.

**Step 1 — Write as column $\times$ row:**

$$\ket{\psi}\bra{\phi} = \begin{pmatrix} \alpha \\ \beta \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} \gamma^* & \delta^* \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Perform the matrix product** (each entry = (row of left)$\times$(column of right)):

$$\ket{\psi}\bra{\phi} = \begin{pmatrix} \alpha\gamma^* & \alpha\delta^* \\ \beta\gamma^* & \beta\delta^* \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Verification using basis projectors:**

Note that:
- $\ket{0}\bra{0} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$
- $\ket{1}\bra{1} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$
- $\ket{0}\bra{1} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$
- $\ket{1}\bra{0} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$

Each basis ket-bra is a **projector** onto a 1-dimensional subspace.

## 3.3 Derivation: Completeness Relation for a Qubit

**Problem:** Show that $I = |0\rangle\langle 0| + |1\rangle\langle 1|$ for a single qubit.

**Step 1 — Evaluate the sum of projectors:**

$$\ket{0}\bra{0} + \ket{1}\bra{1} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Add the matrices:**

$$= \begin{pmatrix} 1+0 & 0+0 \\ 0+0 & 0+1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = I$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

For any state $|\psi\rangle$:

$$\left(\sum_i \ket{i}\bra{i}\right)\ket{\psi} = \sum_i \ket{i}\langle i|\psi\rangle = \sum_i \alpha_i \ket{i} = \ket{\psi}$$

The completeness relation is **inserting an identity** — the most common trick in Dirac-notation calculations.

## 3.4 Derivation: Tensor Product of Two Qubits

**Problem:** Express $|\psi\rangle|\phi\rangle$ where $|\psi\rangle = \alpha|0\rangle + \beta|1\rangle$ and $|\phi\rangle = \gamma|0\rangle + \delta|1\rangle$ as a 4-dimensional vector.

**Step 1 — Expand the product:**

$$\ket{\psi}\ket{\phi} = (\alpha\ket{0} + \beta\ket{1})(\gamma\ket{0} + \delta\ket{1})$$

**Step 2 — Distribute:**

$$= \alpha\gamma\ket{0}\ket{0} + \alpha\delta\ket{0}\ket{1} + \beta\gamma\ket{1}\ket{0} + \beta\delta\ket{1}\ket{1}$$

**Step 3 — Use the shorthand notation** $\ket{ij} = \ket{i}\ket{j} = \ket{i}\otimes\ket{j}$:

$$= \alpha\gamma\ket{00} + \alpha\delta\ket{01} + \beta\gamma\ket{10} + \beta\delta\ket{11}$$

**Step 4 — Vector form** (in lexicographic ordering $|00\rangle, |01\rangle, |10\rangle, |11\rangle$):

$$\ket{\psi}\otimes\ket{\phi} = \begin{pmatrix} \alpha\gamma \\ \alpha\delta \\ \beta\gamma \\ \beta\delta \end{pmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key observation:**
> The dimension is $2 \times 2 = 4$. In general, $n$ qubits require a $2^n$-dimensional vector. This exponential scaling is the origin of quantum computing's expressive power.

## 3.5 Derivation: Action of an Operator in Dirac Form

**Problem:** Show that $\hat{X}|0\rangle = |1\rangle$ and $\hat{X}|1\rangle = |0\rangle$, where $\hat{X}$ is the Pauli-X (NOT) operator.

**Step 1 — Write $\hat{X}$ in Dirac form:**

Using the basis projectors:

$$\hat{X} = \ket{0}\bra{1} + \ket{1}\bra{0}$$

(This is the **bit-flip operator** — it swaps $|0\rangle$ and $|1\rangle$.)

**Step 2 — Compute $\hat{X}|0\rangle$:**

$$\hat{X}\ket{0} = (\ket{0}\bra{1} + \ket{1}\bra{0})\ket{0}$$

Using linearity:

$$= \ket{0}\langle 1|0\rangle + \ket{1}\langle 0|0\rangle$$

Using orthonormality $\langle i|j\rangle = \delta_{ij}$:

$$= \ket{0}\cdot 0 + \ket{1}\cdot 1 = \ket{1}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\hat{X}|1\rangle$:**

$$\hat{X}\ket{1} = (\ket{0}\bra{1} + \ket{1}\bra{0})\ket{1}$$

$$= \ket{0}\langle 1|1\rangle + \ket{1}\langle 0|1\rangle = \ket{0}\cdot 1 + \ket{1}\cdot 0 = \ket{0}$$

**Step 4 — Matrix form verification:**

$$\hat{X} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \hat{X}\begin{pmatrix}1\\0\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}0\\1\end{pmatrix} = \ket{1} \checkmark$$

## 3.6 Derivation: Norm of a Superposition State

**Problem:** Show that $\ket{+} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\ket{0} + \ket{1})$ is normalized.

**Step 1 — Compute the inner product:**

$$\langle + | + \rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\bra{0} + \bra{1}) \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\ket{0} + \ket{1})$$

**Step 2 — Distribute:**

$$= \frac{1}{2}(\langle 0|0\rangle + \langle 0|1\rangle + \langle 1|0\rangle + \langle 1|1\rangle)$$

**Step 3 — Apply orthonormality:**

$$= \frac{1}{2}(1 + 0 + 0 + 1) = \frac{2}{2} = 1 \checkmark$$

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Dirac Notation Operations

| Operation | Dirac Form | Result Type | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bra of a ket | $\ket{\psi}^\dagger = \bra{\psi}$ | Row vector | $\begin{pmatrix}a\\b\end{pmatrix}^\dagger = (a^*, b^*)$ |
| Inner product | $\langle\phi|\psi\rangle$ | Complex scalar | $\gamma^*\alpha + \delta^*\beta$ |
| Outer product | $\ket{\psi}\langle\phi\vert$ | Matrix (operator) | $\begin{pmatrix}\alpha\gamma^* & \alpha\delta^* \\ \beta\gamma^* & \beta\delta^*\end{pmatrix}$ |
| Tensor product | $\ket{a}\otimes\ket{b} = \ket{ab}$ | Vector | $2 \times 2 = 4$-D |
| Sandwich | $\bra{\phi}\hat{A}\ket{\psi}$ | Scalar | $A_{\phi\psi}$ |
| Operator acting | $\hat{A}\ket{\psi} = \ket{\psi'}$ | Vector | $\hat{X}\ket{0}=\ket{1}$ |
| Adjoint | $(\hat{A}\ket{\psi})^\dagger = \bra{\psi}\hat{A}^\dagger$ | Dual vector | $(\bra{0}\hat{X})^\dagger = \hat{X}\ket{0}$ |
| Projector | $\hat{P}_i = \ket{i}\bra{i}$ | Hermitian operator | $\hat{P}_0 + \hat{P}_1 = I$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Dirac Notation Ecosystem — Concept Map

The following Mermaid diagram maps the structural relationships between the core Dirac-notation objects used throughout quantum computing.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Hilbert Space H"]:::space --> B["Ket vector psi"]:::ket
    A --> C["Bra vector psi-dagger"]:::bra
    B --> D["Inner Product bracket phi psi"]:::scalar
    C --> D
    B --> E["Outer Product ket-bra psi phi"]:::operator
    C --> E
    D --> F["Born Rule P_i equals mod psi_i squared"]:::scalar
    E --> G["Completeness Relation sum ket-i bra-i equals I"]:::operator
    G --> H["Operator Action A psi equals lambda psi"]:::eigen
    E --> I["Projector P_i equals ket-i bra-i"]:::operator
    A --> J["Tensor Product H tensor H"]:::space
    J --> K["Multi-qubit State ket-ab"]:::ket
    H --> L["Measurement Outcome lambda"]:::scalar

    classDef space fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,stroke-width:2px,color:#0B3D91
    classDef ket fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025,stroke-width:2px,color:#7B1F1A
    classDef bra fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#188038,stroke-width:2px,color:#0F5223
    classDef scalar fill:#FEF7E0,stroke:#F9AB00,stroke-width:2px,color:#5C4400
    classDef operator fill:#F3E8FD,stroke:#9334E6,stroke-width:2px,color:#4A148C
    classDef eigen fill:#FDECE8,stroke:#E8710A,stroke-width:2px,color:#7A3E0A
```

## 4.2 Bra-Ket Computational Pipeline — Functional Flow

This block diagram shows how a quantum state flows through the Dirac-notation operations during a typical quantum computation.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Input["Input Stage"]
        S0["Computational Basis ket-0"]:::ket
        S1["Computational Basis ket-1"]:::ket
    end

    subgraph Superposition["Superposition Stage"]
        SP["Hadamard Gate H"]:::operator
        SP --> SP1["Plus state ket-plus equals one over root two times ket-0 plus ket-1"]:::ket
    end

    subgraph Algebra["Dirac Algebra Stage"]
        IP["Inner Product bracket plus minus"]:::scalar
        OP["Outer Product ket-plus bra-minus"]:::operator
        TC["Tensor Product H tensor H"]:::operator
        CR["Completeness Relation"]:::operator
    end

    subgraph Measure["Measurement Stage"]
        BR["Born Rule P equals mod bracket i psi squared"]:::scalar
        OUT["Outcome i with probability P"]:::scalar
    end

    S0 --> SP
    S1 --> SP
    SP1 --> IP
    SP1 --> OP
    IP --> BR
    BR --> OUT
    TC --> SP1
    CR --> IP

    classDef ket fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025,stroke-width:2px,color:#7B1F1A
    classDef operator fill:#F3E8FD,stroke:#9334E6,stroke-width:2px,color:#4A148C
    classDef scalar fill:#FEF7E0,stroke:#F9AB00,stroke-width:2px,color:#5C4400
```

## 4.3 Operator Construction via Dirac Notation — Block Architecture

This diagram shows how complex multi-qubit operators are built from elementary Dirac primitives.

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph Prim["Primitive Dirac Objects"]
        P1["ket-0"]:::ket
        P2["ket-1"]:::ket
        P3["bra-0"]:::bra
        P4["bra-1"]:::bra
    end

    subgraph Build["Composite Constructions"]
        C1["Pauli X equals ket-0 bra-1 plus ket-1 bra-0"]:::operator
        C2["Projector P_0 equals ket-0 bra-0"]:::operator
        C3["Identity I equals ket-0 bra-0 plus ket-1 bra-1"]:::operator
        C4["Density Matrix rho equals ket-psi bra-psi"]:::operator
    end

    subgraph Macro["Macro Quantum Operations"]
        M1["Hadamard H equals one over root two times ket-0 plus ket-1 times bra-0 plus ket-0 minus ket-1 times bra-1"]:::operator
        M2["CNOT equals ket-0 bra-0 tensor I plus ket-1 bra-1 tensor X"]:::operator
        M3["Eigen-equation A ket-psi equals lambda ket-psi"]:::eigen
    end

    P1 --> C1
    P2 --> C1
    P3 --> C1
    P4 --> C1
    P1 --> C2
    P3 --> C2
    P1 --> C3
    P2 --> C3
    P3 --> C3
    P4 --> C3
    C1 --> M1
    C2 --> M2
    C3 --> M2
    C4 --> M3

    classDef ket fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025,stroke-width:2px,color:#7B1F1A
    classDef bra fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#188038,stroke-width:2px,color:#0F5223
    classDef operator fill:#F3E8FD,stroke:#9334E6,stroke-width:2px,color:#4A148C
    classDef eigen fill:#FDECE8,stroke:#E8710A,stroke-width:2px,color:#7A3E0A
```

## 4.4 Reading Order for Bracket Expressions — Decision Flow

This diagram helps students parse Dirac expressions correctly — a common source of errors.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Encounter Expression"]:::start --> B{"Is it surrounded by angle brackets?"}:::decide
    B -- "Yes bracket" --> C["Inner Product: bra on left, ket on right"]:::scalar
    B -- "No" --> D{"Is it ket-bra form?"}:::decide
    D -- "Yes" --> E["Outer Product: yields a Matrix Operator"]:::operator
    D -- "No" --> F{"Tensor product symbol present?"}:::decide
    F -- "Yes" --> G["Multi-qubit state: dimensions multiply"]:::ket
    F -- "No" --> H["Check if operator A is between bra and ket"]:::decide
    H -- "Yes" --> I["Sandwich: yields a Scalar matrix element"]:::scalar
    H -- "No" --> J["Operator applied to ket or bra"]:::operator

    classDef start fill:#1A73E8,stroke:#0B3D91,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef decide fill:#FEF7E0,stroke:#F9AB00,stroke-width:2px,color:#5C4400
    classDef scalar fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#188038,stroke-width:2px,color:#0F5223
    classDef operator fill:#F3E8FD,stroke:#9334E6,stroke-width:2px,color:#4A148C
    classDef ket fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025,stroke-width:2px,color:#7B1F1A
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Q: Define Dirac (Bra-Ket) notation. With a suitable example, distinguish between a Ket and a Bra.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Dirac notation, introduced by Paul Dirac in 1939, is a symbolic system for representing quantum states, operators, and inner products using angle brackets $\langle \cdot \mid$ (bra) and $\mid \cdot \rangle$ (ket).

**Ket (1 mark):** A *ket* $|\psi\rangle$ is a column vector representing a quantum state in Hilbert space $\mathcal{H}$. For a qubit, e.g., $|\psi\rangle = \alpha|0\rangle + \beta|1\rangle = \begin{pmatrix}\alpha \\ \beta\end{pmatrix}$.

**Bra (1 mark):** A *bra* $\langle\psi|$ is the Hermitian adjoint (conjugate transpose) of the ket, written as a row vector: $\langle\psi| = (\alpha^*, \beta^*)$. The bra lives in the dual space $\mathcal{H}^*$.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Q: State and explain the Born rule using Dirac notation. Why is normalization essential for a quantum state?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Born rule (2 marks):** If a quantum system is in state $|\psi\rangle = \sum_i \alpha_i |i\rangle$, the probability of obtaining outcome $i$ upon measurement in the orthonormal basis $\{|i\rangle\}$ is:

$$P(i) = \vert \langle i \mid \psi \rangle \vert^2 = \vert \alpha_i \vert^2$$

**Normalization (1 mark):** Because probabilities must sum to unity, a valid quantum state must satisfy $\langle\psi|\psi\rangle = 1$. If $\langle\psi|\psi\rangle = N$, the state is normalized as $|\psi_{\text{norm}}\rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}}|\psi\rangle$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the inner product and outer product in Dirac notation with examples. Show that the completeness relation holds for a single qubit. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the state $|\psi\rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(|0\rangle + i|1\rangle)$, compute:
  (i) $\langle\psi|\psi\rangle$ and verify normalization.
  (ii) The probabilities of measuring $|0\rangle$ and $|1\rangle$.
  (iii) $\langle 0|\psi\rangle$ and $\langle 1|\psi\rangle$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Inner and Outer Product with Completeness Relation (7 marks)**

**Inner Product (2 marks):** The inner product of two states $|\psi\rangle$ and $|\phi\rangle$ is the bracket:

$$\langle\phi|\psi\rangle = \sum_i \phi_i^* \psi_i$$

It is a complex scalar. Example: For $|\psi\rangle = \alpha|0\rangle + \beta|1\rangle$ and $|\phi\rangle = \gamma|0\rangle + \delta|1\rangle$,

$$\langle\phi|\psi\rangle = \gamma^*\alpha + \delta^*\beta$$

**Outer Product (2 marks):** The outer product $|\psi\rangle\langle\phi|$ is an $n \times n$ matrix (operator):

$$\ket{\psi}\bra{\phi} = \begin{pmatrix}\alpha\gamma^* & \alpha\delta^* \\ \beta\gamma^* & \beta\delta^*\end{pmatrix}$$

For example, $|0\rangle\langle 0| = \begin{pmatrix}1 & 0\\0 & 0\end{pmatrix}$ is the projector onto $|0\rangle$.

**Completeness Relation (3 marks):** For a single qubit:

$$\sum_{i=0}^{1}\ket{i}\bra{i} = \ket{0}\bra{0} + \ket{1}\bra{1} = \begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&0\end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix}0&0\\0&1\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&1\end{pmatrix} = I$$

This identity operator acts on any state: $I|\psi\rangle = |\psi\rangle$, confirming completeness.

---

**(b) State Computations (7 marks)**

Given: $|\psi\rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(|0\rangle + i|1\rangle)$, so $\alpha = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \beta = \frac{i}{\sqrt{2}}$.

**(i) Normalization verification (3 marks):**

$$\langle\psi|\psi\rangle = \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^*\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) + \left(\frac{i}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^*\left(\frac{i}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$$

$$= \frac{1}{2} + \frac{(-i)(i)}{2} = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{-i^2}{2} = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2} = 1 \checkmark$$

**(ii) Measurement probabilities (2 marks):**

$$P(0) = \vert\langle 0|\psi\rangle\vert^2 = \left\vert \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \right\vert^2 = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$P(1) = \vert\langle 1|\psi\rangle\vert^2 = \left\vert \frac{i}{\sqrt{2}} \right\vert^2 = \frac{1}{2}$$

Sum = 1 ✓

**(iii) Inner products (2 marks):**

$$\langle 0|\psi\rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \quad \langle 1|\psi\rangle = \frac{i}{\sqrt{2}}$$

---

### Question B — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Define the tensor product in Dirac notation. Construct the two-qubit state $|\psi\rangle \otimes |\phi\rangle$ where $|\psi\rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(|0\rangle + |1\rangle)$ and $|\phi\rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(|0\rangle - |1\rangle)$. Verify that the resulting state is normalized. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Express the Pauli-X operator $\hat{X}$, the Hadamard operator $\hat{H}$, and the identity $\hat{I}$ in Dirac notation for a single qubit. Show using Dirac algebra that $\hat{X}\hat{X} = \hat{I}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Tensor Product Construction (7 marks)**

**Definition (2 marks):** The tensor product combines two Hilbert spaces $\mathcal{H}_1 \otimes \mathcal{H}_2$ into a joint space. For kets:

$$\ket{\psi}\otimes\ket{\phi} \equiv \ket{\psi\phi} = \ket{\psi}\ket{\phi}$$

**Expand (3 marks):**

$$\ket{\psi}\otimes\ket{\phi} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\ket{0}+\ket{1}) \otimes \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\ket{0}-\ket{1})$$

$$= \frac{1}{2}\left[\ket{0}\otimes\ket{0} - \ket{0}\otimes\ket{1} + \ket{1}\otimes\ket{0} - \ket{1}\otimes\ket{1}\right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{2}\left[\ket{00} - \ket{01} + \ket{10} - \ket{11}\right]$$

**Verification of normalization (2 marks):**

$$\langle\psi\phi|\psi\phi\rangle = \frac{1}{4}(1 + 1 + 1 + 1) = 1 \checkmark$$

(The state is the well-known Bell-like state $|\Phi^-\rangle$ up to a phase.)

---

**(b) Dirac Form of Single-Qubit Operators (7 marks)**

**Pauli-X in Dirac form (2 marks):**

$$\hat{X} = \ket{0}\bra{1} + \ket{1}\bra{0}$$

**Hadamard in Dirac form (2 marks):**

$$\hat{H} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\left[(\ket{0}+\ket{1})\bra{0} + (\ket{0}-\ket{1})\bra{1}\right] = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sum_{i,j\in\{0,1\}} (-1)^{ij}\ket{i}\bra{j}$$

**Identity (1 mark):** $\hat{I} = \ket{0}\bra{0} + \ket{1}\bra{1}$

**Show $\hat{X}\hat{X} = \hat{I}$ (2 marks):**

$$\hat{X}\hat{X} = (\ket{0}\bra{1} + \ket{1}\bra{0})(\ket{0}\bra{1} + \ket{1}\bra{0})$$

$$= \ket{0}\langle 1|0\rangle\bra{1} + \ket{0}\langle 1|1\rangle\bra{0} + \ket{1}\langle 0|0\rangle\bra{1} + \ket{1}\langle 0|1\rangle\bra{0}$$

Using orthonormality $\langle i|j\rangle = \delta_{ij}$:

$$= \ket{0}\cdot 0\cdot\bra{1} + \ket{0}\cdot 1\cdot\bra{0} + \ket{1}\cdot 1\cdot\bra{1} + \ket{1}\cdot 0\cdot\bra{0}$$

$$= \ket{0}\bra{0} + \ket{1}\bra{1} = \hat{I} \checkmark$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Bra and Ket order** (–2 marks): Students often write $\langle\psi|\phi\rangle$ when they mean $\langle\phi|\psi\rangle$. The bra is on the LEFT; complex conjugation acts on the LEFT vector's coefficients.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the conjugate on inner products** (–2 marks): $\langle\phi|\psi\rangle = \sum \phi_i^* \psi_i$, NOT $\sum \phi_i \psi_i$. The conjugate goes with the bra.
>
> 3. **Mixing up inner and outer products** (–1 mark): $\langle\phi|\psi\rangle$ is a SCALAR (number). $|\phi\rangle\langle\psi|$ is a MATRIX (operator). These are dimensionally different.
>
> 4. **Missing the normalization factor** (–2 marks): Whenever amplitudes are given, always check or enforce $\sum |\alpha_i|^2 = 1$. Forgetting $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ is a frequent mistake.
>
> 5. **Not applying orthonormality** $\langle i|j\rangle = \delta_{ij}$ (–2 marks): This identity is the workhorse of Dirac algebra — without it, sandwich calculations cannot simplify.
>
> 6. **Writing kets as scalars** (–1 mark): A ket is a VECTOR, not a number. Always represent it as a column when computing.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Dirac Notation**

- ✅ **Ket $|\psi\rangle$** = column vector (state); **Bra $\langle\psi|$** = row vector (dual state).
- ✅ **Inner product** $\langle\phi|\psi\rangle$ = scalar; **Outer product** $|\phi\rangle\langle\psi|$ = operator (matrix).
- ✅ **Orthonormality:** $\langle i|j\rangle = \delta_{ij}$ (Kronecker delta).
- ✅ **Completeness:** $I = \sum_i |i\rangle\langle i|$ — use it to "insert" identities.
- ✅ **Born rule:** $P(i) = |\langle i|\psi\rangle|^2$ — measurement probability.
- ✅ **Hermitian adjoint:** $\langle\psi|A = (A|\psi\rangle)^\dagger$.
- ✅ **Tensor product:** $|\psi\rangle|\phi\rangle = |\psi\rangle \otimes |\phi\rangle$ for multi-qubit states; dimensions multiply.
- ✅ **Eigen-equation:** $A|\psi\rangle = \lambda|\psi\rangle$ — Dirac form of eigenvalue problems.
- ✅ **Normalization:** Always enforce $\langle\psi|\psi\rangle = 1$ for valid quantum states.
- ✅ **Matrix element:** $A_{ij} = \langle i|A|j\rangle$ — basis representation of operators.
- ✅ **Common operators in Dirac form:**
  - $I = |0\rangle\langle 0| + |1\rangle\langle 1|$
  - $X = |0\rangle\langle 1| + |1\rangle\langle 0|$
  - $Z = |0\rangle\langle 0| - |1\rangle\langle 1|$
  - $H = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\left[(|0\rangle+|1\rangle)\langle 0| + (|0\rangle-|1\rangle)\langle 1|\right]$
- ✅ **Qiskit mapping:** In Python, `qc.statevector()` returns the ket coefficients; `np.kron()` implements the tensor product.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:**
> Whenever a question asks to "show," "verify," or "compute using Dirac notation," ALWAYS:
> (1) Write the ket/bra form first,
> (2) Apply orthonormality $\langle i|j\rangle = \delta_{ij}$ to simplify,
> (3) Convert to matrix form ONLY as a final check.
> Skipping Step (2) is where most marks are lost.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Qubits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET863 - QUANTUM COMPUTING WITH PYTHON
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Qubits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Qubits

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **quantum bit (qubit)** is the fundamental unit of quantum information, defined as the normalized quantum-mechanical two-state system residing in a two-dimensional complex Hilbert space $\mathcal{H} \cong \mathbb{C}^2$. Mathematically, a pure-state qubit is represented as a linear superposition of two orthonormal computational basis states $\vert 0 \rangle$ and $\vert 1 \rangle$:

$$\vert \psi \rangle = \alpha \vert 0 \rangle + \beta \vert 1 \rangle$$

where the complex probability amplitudes $\alpha, \beta \in \mathbb{C}$ satisfy the **normalization constraint** $\vert \alpha \vert^2 + \vert \beta \vert^2 = 1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim standard):** A qubit is *the quantum analogue of the classical bit; it is a two-level quantum system whose state is a unit vector in a two-dimensional complex Hilbert space, and it can exist in a coherent linear superposition of its basis states until measurement collapses it probabilistically.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Spinning Coin

Imagine a classical coin lying on a table. It is unambiguously either **Heads (H)** or **Tails (T)** — there is no in-between state. This is your *classical bit*, locked into one of two discrete positions.

Now imagine the coin **spinning in mid-air on its edge**. While it spins, it is *not* heads *and* it is *not* tails — it is a genuinely new state called a **superposition**. We can describe it as some weighted blend: *70% heads-feel, 30% tails-feel*. The moment it lands on the table, it collapses to either H or T with a probability determined by those weights.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Spinning Coin ≠ the Qubit Exactly.** A real qubit is richer: the superposition carries not only amplitudes but also a relative *phase* — an invisible, complex-valued tilt that has no classical counterpart. Still, the spinning coin is the closest everyday intuition we have.

### 1.3 The Computational Basis States

The two canonical basis vectors of $\mathbb{C}^2$ are given in Dirac (bra-ket) notation:

$$\vert 0 \rangle = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad \vert 1 \rangle = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

These are identical in physical structure to classical 0 and 1 but obey the rules of quantum mechanics, enabling superposition, interference, and entanglement.

### 1.4 Physical Realizations (KTU 2024 Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Qubits are *abstract mathematical objects*. In hardware, they may be realized as: the **spin-½** of an electron ($\uparrow$ / $\downarrow$), the **two lowest energy levels** of a superconducting transmon circuit, the **polarization states** of a single photon (horizontal/vertical), or **trapped ion** internal electronic levels. The unifying feature is *exactly two accessible, addressable quantum states*.

### 1.5 Key Metrics and Constants

| Metric / Quantity | Symbol | Value / Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Planck's constant | $\hbar$ | $\approx 1.054 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s |
| Number of qubits | $n$ | Determines Hilbert-space dimension $2^n$ |
| Dimension of $n$-qubit state space | $\dim(\mathcal{H}^{\otimes n})$ | $2^n$ complex amplitudes |
| Probability sum (normalization) | $\sum \vert c_i \vert^2$ | $= 1$ (always) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Qubit State

The state $\vert \psi \rangle = \alpha \vert 0 \rangle + \beta \vert 1 \rangle$ has the column-vector form:

$$\vert \psi \rangle = \begin{pmatrix} \alpha \\ \beta \end{pmatrix}, \quad \alpha, \beta \in \mathbb{C}$$

The probability of obtaining the outcome $0$ upon measurement is $\vert \alpha \vert^2$, and the probability of obtaining $1$ is $\vert \beta \vert^2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why complex numbers?** Because a qubit's *relative phase* $e^{i\phi}$ between $\alpha$ and $\beta$ is physically observable (it controls interference). A purely real 2D probability vector would lose this crucial information.

## 2.2 Geometric Interpretation — The Bloch Sphere

Every pure qubit state can be uniquely visualized as a point on the surface of a unit sphere in $\mathbb{R}^3$, called the **Bloch sphere**:

$$\vert \psi \rangle = \cos\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\vert 0 \rangle + e^{i\phi}\sin\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\vert 1 \rangle$$

- $\theta \in [0, \pi]$ is the **polar angle** (measured from the positive $z$-axis).
- $\phi \in [0, 2\pi)$ is the **azimuthal angle** (measured in the $xy$-plane).
- The factor $\frac{\theta}{2}$ (not $\theta$) is a deliberate mathematical choice ensuring normalization.

### 2.3 The Six Cardinal States on the Bloch Sphere

| Polar Coordinate $(\theta, \phi)$ | State $\vert \psi \rangle$ | Bloch-Sphere Point |
|---|---|---|
| $(0, \, \cdot)$ | $\vert 0 \rangle$ | North pole $(0,0,1)$ |
| $(\pi, \, \cdot)$ | $\vert 1 \rangle$ | South pole $(0,0,-1)$ |
| $(\tfrac{\pi}{2}, 0)$ | $\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\vert 0 \rangle + \vert 1 \rangle) \equiv \vert + \rangle$ | $+x$-axis |
| $(\tfrac{\pi}{2}, \pi)$ | $\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\vert 0 \rangle - \vert 1 \rangle) \equiv \vert - \rangle$ | $-x$-axis |
| $(\tfrac{\pi}{2}, \tfrac{\pi}{2})$ | $\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\vert 0 \rangle + i\vert 1 \rangle) \equiv \vert +i \rangle$ | $+y$-axis |
| $(\tfrac{\pi}{2}, \tfrac{3\pi}{2})$ | $\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\vert 0 \rangle - i\vert 1 \rangle) \equiv \vert -i \rangle$ | $-y$-axis |

## 2.4 Multi-Qubit Systems and Tensor Products

A register of $n$ qubits lives in the Hilbert space $\mathcal{H}^{\otimes n} = \underbrace{\mathbb{C}^2 \otimes \cdots \otimes \mathbb{C}^2}_{n \text{ times}}$, which has dimension $2^n$. The $2^n$ computational basis states are formed by **tensor (Kronecker) products** of single-qubit basis vectors:

$$\vert ab \rangle \equiv \vert a \rangle \otimes \vert b \rangle = \begin{pmatrix} a_0 b_0 \\ a_0 b_1 \\ a_1 b_0 \\ a_1 b_1 \end{pmatrix}$$

For two qubits $\vert \psi \rangle = \alpha \vert 00 \rangle + \beta \vert 01 \rangle + \gamma \vert 10 \rangle + \delta \vert 11 \rangle$, the normalization becomes:

$$\vert \alpha \vert^2 + \vert \beta \vert^2 + \vert \gamma \vert^2 + \vert \delta \vert^2 = 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Entanglement footnote (relevant to Module 1 discussion):** A two-qubit state is *separable* if $\vert \psi \rangle = \vert \psi_A \rangle \otimes \vert \psi_B \rangle$; otherwise it is *entangled*. The Bell state $\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\vert 00 \rangle + \vert 11 \rangle)$ is the canonical entangled example — it cannot be written as a product of two single-qubit states.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\vert \psi \rangle = \alpha \vert 0 \rangle + \beta \vert 1 \rangle$ | General single-qubit state |
| 2 | $\vert \alpha \vert^2 + \vert \beta \vert^2 = 1$ | Normalization (Born rule prerequisite) |
| 3 | $\Pr(0) = \vert \alpha \vert^2, \quad \Pr(1) = \vert \beta \vert^2$ | Measurement probabilities (Born rule) |
| 4 | $\vert \psi \rangle = e^{i\gamma}\vert \psi \rangle$ | Global phase is *unobservable* |
| 5 | $\alpha \rightarrow e^{i\phi}\alpha$ | *Relative* phase *is* observable |
| 6 | $\theta \in [0,\pi], \; \phi \in [0, 2\pi)$ | Bloch-sphere angular range |
| 7 | $\dim(\mathcal{H}^{\otimes n}) = 2^n$ | $n$-qubit Hilbert-space dimension |
| 8 | $\langle 0 \vert 0 \rangle = \langle 1 \vert 1 \rangle = 1$, $\langle 0 \vert 1 \rangle = 0$ | Orthonormality of computational basis |
| 9 | $\vert \psi^{\otimes n} \rangle$ | $n$-fold tensor product (non-entangled register) |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cryptography:** Shor's algorithm breaks RSA using qubit registers of size $n \approx 2048$; requires physical qubits in the thousands (with error correction).
- **Quantum sensing:** Qubit states enable magnetometry at sensitivities beyond the standard quantum limit.
- **Optimization:** Variational quantum eigensolvers (VQE) encode molecular Hamiltonians into qubit Hamiltonians via fermion-to-qubit mappings (e.g., Jordan–Wigner).
- **Communication:** Quantum key distribution (BB84) directly exploits the four cardinal Bloch-sphere states $\{\vert 0 \rangle, \vert 1 \rangle, \vert + \rangle, \vert - \rangle\}$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — Why the Bloch Sphere Polar Angle Is $\theta / 2$

**Claim:** A pure state $\vert \psi \rangle = \cos(\theta/2)\vert 0 \rangle + e^{i\phi}\sin(\theta/2)\vert 1 \rangle$ has *unit norm* for all real $\theta, \phi$.

**Step 1 — Compute the inner product $\langle \psi \vert \psi \rangle$.** The dual (bra) of a complex column vector is its conjugate transpose:

$$\langle \psi \vert = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\!\left(\tfrac{\theta}{2}\right) e^{-i\cdot 0}, & e^{-i\phi}\sin\!\left(\tfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \end{pmatrix}^*_{\text{inner}} = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\!\left(\tfrac{\theta}{2}\right), & e^{-i\phi}\sin\!\left(\tfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \end{pmatrix}$$

The conjugate transpose (row) form is:

$$\langle \psi \vert = \cos\!\left(\tfrac{\theta}{2}\right)\langle 0 \vert + e^{-i\phi}\sin\!\left(\tfrac{\theta}{2}\right)\langle 1 \vert$$

**Step 2 — Multiply $\langle \psi \vert$ with $\vert \psi \rangle$.** Use orthonormality $\langle 0 \vert 0 \rangle = \langle 1 \vert 1 \rangle = 1$ and $\langle 0 \vert 1 \rangle = \langle 1 \vert 0 \rangle = 0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\langle \psi \vert \psi \rangle &= \left[\cos\!\left(\tfrac{\theta}{2}\right)\langle 0 \vert + e^{-i\phi}\sin\!\left(\tfrac{\theta}{2}\right)\langle 1 \vert\right]\left[\cos\!\left(\tfrac{\theta}{2}\right)\vert 0 \rangle + e^{i\phi}\sin\!\left(\tfrac{\theta}{2}\right)\vert 1 \rangle\right] \\
&= \cos^2\!\left(\tfrac{\theta}{2}\right)\langle 0 \vert 0 \rangle + \cos\!\left(\tfrac{\theta}{2}\right)e^{i\phi}\sin\!\left(\tfrac{\theta}{2}\right)\langle 0 \vert 1 \rangle \\
&\quad + e^{-i\phi}\sin\!\left(\tfrac{\theta}{2}\right)\cos\!\left(\tfrac{\theta}{2}\right)\langle 1 \vert 0 \rangle + e^{-i\phi}e^{i\phi}\sin^2\!\left(\tfrac{\theta}{2}\right)\langle 1 \vert 1 \rangle \\
&= \cos^2\!\left(\tfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \cdot 1 + 0 + 0 + \sin^2\!\left(\tfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \cdot 1 \\
&= \cos^2\!\left(\tfrac{\theta}{2}\right) + \sin^2\!\left(\tfrac{\theta}{2}\right) = 1 \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation.** The half-angle guarantees the trigonometric Pythagorean identity $\cos^2 x + \sin^2 x = 1$ enforces normalization automatically.

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — Born-Rule Probabilities on the Bloch Sphere

**Claim:** For $\vert \psi \rangle = \cos(\theta/2)\vert 0 \rangle + e^{i\phi}\sin(\theta/2)\vert 1 \rangle$, the measurement probabilities are:

$$\Pr(0) = \cos^2\!\left(\tfrac{\theta}{2}\right), \qquad \Pr(1) = \sin^2\!\left(\tfrac{\theta}{2}\right)$$

**Step 1 — Identify the amplitudes.** From the state expansion, $\alpha = \cos(\theta/2)$ and $\beta = e^{i\phi}\sin(\theta/2)$.

**Step 2 — Compute $\vert \alpha \vert^2$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\vert \alpha \vert^2 &= \alpha^* \alpha = \cos\!\left(\tfrac{\theta}{2}\right)\cos\!\left(\tfrac{\theta}{2}\right) = \cos^2\!\left(\tfrac{\theta}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\vert \beta \vert^2$.** Note that $\vert e^{i\phi} \vert^2 = e^{-i\phi}e^{i\phi} = 1$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\vert \beta \vert^2 &= \vert e^{i\phi} \vert^2 \sin^2\!\left(\tfrac{\theta}{2}\right) = (1)\sin^2\!\left(\tfrac{\theta}{2}\right) = \sin^2\!\left(\tfrac{\theta}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Verify normalization.**

$$\Pr(0) + \Pr(1) = \cos^2\!\left(\tfrac{\theta}{2}\right) + \sin^2\!\left(\tfrac{\theta}{2}\right) = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Physical reading.** The polar angle $\theta$ alone (not $\phi$) controls measurement probabilities; $\phi$ instead governs *interference* between branches.

## 3.3 Derivation #3 — Tensor Product of Two Qubits

**Claim:** $\vert + \rangle \otimes \vert 0 \rangle = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\vert 00 \rangle + \vert 10 \rangle)$.

**Step 1 — Expand each qubit.**

$$\vert + \rangle = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\vert 0 \rangle + \vert 1 \rangle), \qquad \vert 0 \rangle = \vert 0 \rangle$$

**Step 2 — Apply the bilinearity of the tensor product.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\vert + \rangle \otimes \vert 0 \rangle &= \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\vert 0 \rangle + \vert 1 \rangle) \otimes \vert 0 \rangle \\
&= \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\left[(\vert 0 \rangle \otimes \vert 0 \rangle) + (\vert 1 \rangle \otimes \vert 0 \rangle)\right] \\
&= \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\vert 00 \rangle + \vert 10 \rangle)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Verify normalization.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\langle +0 \vert +0 \rangle &= \tfrac{1}{2}(\langle 00 \vert + \langle 10 \vert)(\vert 00 \rangle + \vert 10 \rangle) \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2}(\langle 00 \vert 00 \rangle + \langle 10 \vert 10 \rangle) \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2}(1 + 1) = 1 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.4 Derivation #4 — Average Value (Expectation) of Pauli-$Z$ on a Pure Qubit

The Pauli-$Z$ operator is $Z = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix} = \vert 0 \rangle\langle 0 \vert - \vert 1 \rangle\langle 1 \vert$.

**Step 1 — Compute $\langle Z \rangle$ on $\vert \psi \rangle = \alpha \vert 0 \rangle + \beta \vert 1 \rangle$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\langle Z \rangle &= \langle \psi \vert Z \vert \psi \rangle \\
&= (\alpha^* \langle 0 \vert + \beta^* \langle 1 \vert)(\alpha \vert 0 \rangle - \beta \vert 1 \rangle) \\
&= \alpha^*\alpha \langle 0 \vert 0 \rangle - \alpha^*\beta \langle 0 \vert 1 \rangle + \beta^*\alpha \langle 1 \vert 0 \rangle - \beta^*\beta \langle 1 \vert 1 \rangle \\
&= \vert \alpha \vert^2 - \vert \beta \vert^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Connect to Bloch coordinates.** Substituting $\alpha = \cos(\theta/2), \, \beta = e^{i\phi}\sin(\theta/2)$:

$$\langle Z \rangle = \cos^2\!\left(\tfrac{\theta}{2}\right) - \sin^2\!\left(\tfrac{\theta}{2}\right) = \cos \theta$$

**Conclusion:** $\langle Z \rangle = \cos\theta$ is exactly the $z$-component of the Bloch vector, confirming geometric consistency.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Probability of Each Cardinal Outcome

| Input State $\vert \psi \rangle$ | $\Pr(\text{measure } 0)$ | $\Pr(\text{measure } 1)$ | Bloch Vector |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\vert 0 \rangle$ | $1$ | $0$ | $(0, 0, +1)$ |
| $\vert 1 \rangle$ | $0$ | $1$ | $(0, 0, -1)$ |
| $\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\vert 0 \rangle + \vert 1 \rangle)$ | $1/2$ | $1/2$ | $(+1, 0, 0)$ |
| $\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\vert 0 \rangle - \vert 1 \rangle)$ | $1/2$ | $1/2$ | $(-1, 0, 0)$ |
| $\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\vert 0 \rangle + i\vert 1 \rangle)$ | $1/2$ | $1/2$ | $(0, +1, 0)$ |
| $\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\vert 0 \rangle - i\vert 1 \rangle)$ | $1/2$ | $1/2$ | $(0, -1, 0)$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bloch Sphere — Functional Architecture Flow

The following diagram maps the *flow of information* through a single-qubit system, including the mathematical mapping to the Bloch sphere and the measurement apparatus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Classical Bit 0 or 1"]:::classical --> B["State Preparation Unit"]
    A2["Angles theta and phi"]:::params --> B
    B --> C["Qubit State ket psi equal to cos half theta ket 0 plus exp i phi sin half theta ket 1"]
    C --> D["Normalization Check: mod alpha squared plus mod beta squared equals 1"]
    D -->|Valid| E["Bloch Sphere Mapping: 3D Unit Vector on Sphere Surface"]
    D -->|Invalid| ERR["Reject State: Return Error to User"]:::error
    E --> F["Apply Quantum Gates U"]
    F --> G["Measurement Apparatus"]
    G --> H{"Born Rule Sampling"}
    H -->|Probability mod alpha squared| I["Outcome: Classical Bit 0"]
    H -->|Probability mod beta squared| J["Outcome: Classical Bit 1"]

    classDef classical fill:#e8f0ff,stroke:#3366cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#0a1f44
    classDef params fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#ff8800,stroke-width:2px,color:#663300
    classDef error fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#660000
```

## 4.2 Multi-Qubit Tensor Product Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1["Qubit 1: ket psi 1 in C2"] --> T["Tensor Product Operator"]
    Q2["Qubit 2: ket psi 2 in C2"] --> T
    Q3["Qubit n: ket psi n in C2"] --> T
    T --> R["Composite Register in C raised to 2n"]
    R --> B1["Computational Basis: ket 00, ket 01, ..., ket 11"]
    R --> B2["Entangled States: Bell, GHZ, W"]
    R --> B3["Separable States: Product Form"]

    classDef qubitBox fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px,color:#003c40
    classDef regBox fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px,color:#1a0033
    classDef basisBox fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#ff6f00,stroke-width:1px,color:#3e2723
```

## 4.3 Measurement Outcome Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Input State ket psi equal to alpha ket 0 plus beta ket 1"] --> N{"Normalization Satisfied: mod alpha squared plus mod beta squared equals 1"}
    N -->|No| X1["Invalid State: Reject"]
    N -->|Yes| P["Compute Probabilities: P0 equals mod alpha squared, P1 equals mod beta squared"]
    P --> R["Draw Random Number r uniformly in 0,1"]
    R --> C{"r less than P0"}
    C -->|Yes| OUT0["Collapse to ket 0: Output Classical 0"]
    C -->|No| OUT1["Collapse to ket 1: Output Classical 1"]
    OUT0 --> END["State Destroyed: No Further Superposition"]
    OUT1 --> END
```

## 4.4 Global Phase vs. Relative Phase — Distinction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PH["Qubit Phase Factor exp i phi"] --> Q1{"Is phi attached to BOTH amplitudes equally?"}
    Q1 -->|Yes| GP["GLOBAL PHASE: Physically Unobservable; State ket psi and exp i gamma ket psi are Identical"]
    Q1 -->|No| RP["RELATIVE PHASE: Physically Observable; Controls Interference Pattern"]
    GP --> EX1["Example: 2 times ket 0 equals ket 0 (Up to a Scalar)"]
    RP --> EX2["Example: ket plus equals 1 over sqrt 2 times ket 0 plus ket 1, versus ket minus equals 1 over sqrt 2 times ket 0 minus ket 1: Orthogonal, Distinguishable"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Bloch Sphere Diagram Note:** Because the Bloch sphere is a continuous 3D manifold (a curved surface in $\mathbb{R}^3$), it is rendered here as a functional block architecture rather than a literal drawing. The reader should imagine a unit sphere centered at the origin with $\vert 0 \rangle$ at the top, $\vert 1 \rangle$ at the bottom, and the equatorial states $\vert \pm \rangle$, $\vert \pm i \rangle$ on the $x$ and $y$ axes respectively.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
