# Module 4 — Quantum Hardware Technologies
## Part A: Superconducting Qubits & Trapped Ions

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Introduction to Quantum Hardware Platforms

A **quantum hardware platform** is a physical realization of a two-level quantum system (qubit) that can be initialized, manipulated, and read out with high fidelity, while maintaining quantum coherence long enough to perform meaningful quantum computations. Different platforms compete across axes such as **coherence time (T₁, T₂)**, **gate fidelity**, **scalability**, **connectivity**, and **operating temperature**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Quantum hardware refers to the physical substrate upon which qubits are engineered. Two of the most mature and commercially pursued platforms are **Superconducting Qubits** (solid-state, microwave-controlled) and **Trapped Ion Qubits** (atomic, laser-controlled). Each exploits a distinct quantum degree of freedom — phase/charge in superconducting circuits, and electronic/internal states of individual ions in ion traps.

### 1.2 Superconducting Qubits — Definition & Analogy

**Formal Definition:**
A superconducting qubit is a quantum bit realized using a superconducting LC oscillator circuit shunted by a non-linear element called a **Josephson Junction** (JJ). The non-linearity of the JJ removes the degeneracy of energy levels in the harmonic oscillator, allowing isolation of the lowest two energy levels as the logical |0⟩ and |1⟩ states.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Tuning Fork in a Freezer":**
Imagine a tuning fork (an LC resonator) that would normally ring forever at one frequency. Place it inside a special "freezer" cooled to **15 mK** (colder than outer space). Replace one rigid wall with a "springy" Josephson junction that makes the system **anharmonic** (each level spaced differently). Now you can address just one note of the tuning fork with microwave pulses — that single note is your qubit. The freezer suppresses thermal noise; the springiness gives you a controllable two-level system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constants & Metrics for Superconducting Qubits:**
> - Operating temperature: **T ≈ 10–20 mK** (dilution refrigerator)
> - Qubit transition frequency: **f₀₁ ≈ 3–10 GHz** (microwave regime)
> - Coherence time: **T₁, T₂ ≈ 50–300 μs** (state-of-the-art)
> - Single-qubit gate fidelity: **> 99.9%**
> - Two-qubit gate fidelity: **> 99%** (e.g., Cross-Resonance, CZ gates)

### 1.3 Trapped Ion Qubits — Definition & Analogy

**Formal Definition:**
A trapped ion qubit uses individual charged atoms (ions), confined in vacuum by **electromagnetic fields** (Paul or Penning traps), whose **internal electronic states** (typically hyperfine or Zeeman sub-levels) serve as the |0⟩ and |1⟩ qubit states. Quantum gates are implemented by **laser pulses** or microwave fields that drive transitions between these internal states, while **collective vibrational modes** (phonons) of the ion chain mediate two-qubit interactions.

**Conceptual Analogy — "Marbles in a Magnetic Egg Carton":**
Imagine tiny charged marbles (ions) floating in a vacuum chamber. An invisible "magnetic egg carton" (the RF electric field of a Paul trap) holds each marble in a precise spot, but lets them jiggle like a chain of beads on a string. Two energy levels inside each marble (e.g., a hyperfine clock transition) act as the qubit. You "speak" to each marble with finely-tuned laser pointers — red laser flips the qubit, blue laser makes neighbouring marbles "talk" to each other through their shared jiggle (phonon mode).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constants & Metrics for Trapped Ions:**
> - Operating temperature (ion motional): **T ≈ 10⁻⁶ K** (after Doppler/resolved-sideband cooling)
> - Qubit transition frequency: **f₀₁ ≈ 10⁻³–10⁰ GHz** (e.g., ⁴⁰Ca⁺ at 3.2 GHz, ¹⁷¹Yb⁺ at 12.6 GHz)
> - Coherence time: **T₂ > 1 second** (hyperfine clock qubits, record > 5500 s)
> - Single-qubit gate fidelity: **> 99.99%**
> - Two-qubit gate fidelity: **> 99.9%** (e.g., Mølmer–Sørensen gate)

### 1.4 Comparative Snapshot

| Feature | Superconducting Qubits | Trapped Ion Qubits |
|---|---|---|
| Physical substrate | Solid-state (Nb, Al on Si) | Atomic (⁴⁰Ca⁺, ¹⁷¹Yb⁺, ⁸⁸Sr⁺, ¹³⁷Ba⁺) |
| Qubit degree of freedom | Phase / charge / flux | Internal electronic states |
| Control mechanism | Microwave pulses | Laser pulses / microwaves |
| Operating temperature | ~ 15 mK | Room temperature vacuum chamber |
| Coherence time | ~ 100 μs | > 1 s (can be > 10³ s) |
| Gate speed | ~ 10–200 ns | ~ 1–200 μs |
| Connectivity | Local (nearest-neighbor) | All-to-all (shared phonon bus) |
| Leading vendors | IBM, Google, Rigetti | IonQ, Quantinuum, Oxford Ionics |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Superconducting Qubits — Operational Principles

#### 2.1.1 The Josephson Junction

A Josephson Junction consists of two superconductors separated by a thin insulating barrier (≈ 1–2 nm). Cooper pairs tunnel coherently across the barrier, producing two fundamental relations:

- **Josephson Current Relation (1st equation):**
  $I = I_c \sin(\varphi)$
  
  where $I_c$ is the critical current and $\varphi$ is the superconducting phase difference.

- **Josephson Voltage Relation (2nd equation):**
  $\dot{\varphi} = \dfrac{2eV}{\hbar}$

#### 2.1.2 The Josephson Hamiltonian

The energy stored in a JJ is the Josephson coupling energy:

$$\boxed{E_J = \frac{\hbar I_c}{2e} = \frac{\Phi_0 I_c}{2\pi}}$$

where $\Phi_0 = h/2e \approx 2.067 \times 10^{-15}$ Wb is the **superconducting flux quantum**.

The full Hamiltonian of a JJ-shunted oscillator (no offset charge yet) is:

$$H = 4 E_C (\hat{n} - n_g)^2 - E_J \cos(\hat{\varphi})$$

where:
- $E_C = \dfrac{e^2}{2C_{\Sigma}}$ is the **charging energy** of the total capacitance
- $\hat{n}$ is the **Cooper-pair number operator** (conjugate to $\hat{\varphi}$: $[\hat{\varphi}, \hat{n}] = i$)
- $n_g = Q_g / 2e$ is the **offset charge** (gate-controlled)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a JJ makes a qubit:** A plain LC oscillator has **equally spaced** energy levels (harmonic), so a microwave pulse would excite many transitions at once. The cosine non-linearity of $-E_J \cos(\hat{\varphi})$ makes the levels **anharmonic**, allowing addressability of just the |0⟩ ↔ |1⟩ transition.

#### 2.1.2.1 Charge Qubit (Cooper Pair Box)

The **Cooper-pair box** operates in the regime $E_C \gg E_J$. The number of Cooper pairs $n$ on the island is a good quantum number. Near $n_g = 1/2$, the lowest two charge states $\vert n \rangle$ and $\vert n+1 \rangle$ form the qubit:

$$H_{\text{CPB}} \approx -\frac{E_J}{2}\sigma_x + E_C (1 - 2 n_g)\sigma_z$$

This qubit is **charge-sensitive** and suffers from charge noise — it is rarely used in isolation today.

#### 2.1.2.2 Transmon Qubit (the dominant design)

The **transmon** operates in the **opposite** regime:

$$\boxed{\frac{E_J}{E_C} \gg 1 \quad \text{(typically } 50 - 200\text{)}}$$

Increasing $E_J/E_C$ exponentially suppresses charge noise sensitivity but only polynomially reduces the anharmonicity. The transmon Hamiltonian is well-approximated by a **Duffing oscillator**:

$$H_{\text{transmon}} \approx \hbar \omega_q \hat{a}^\dagger \hat{a} + \frac{\hbar \alpha}{2} \hat{a}^\dagger \hat{a}^\dagger \hat{a}\hat{a}$$

where $\alpha < 0$ is the **anharmonicity** (typically $\alpha \approx -200$ to $-300$ MHz). The transmon has become the workhorse of IBM, Google, and Rigetti.

#### 2.1.2.3 Flux Qubit & Phase Qubit

- **Flux qubit (RF-SQUID):** A superconducting loop interrupted by 1–3 JJs. Qubit states are persistent currents circulating clockwise or counter-clockwise (flux states). Tunable via external magnetic flux $\Phi_{ext}$.
- **Phase qubit:** A current-biased JJ; the phase difference across the junction plays the role of the coordinate. Operates at $E_J \gg E_C$.

#### 2.1.3 Single- & Two-Qubit Gates

- **Single-qubit gates** are driven by microwave tones resonant with the |0⟩ → |1⟩ transition:
$$H_{\text{drive}}(t) = \hbar \Omega (e^{i\omega_d t} \sigma_+ + e^{-i\omega_d t} \sigma_-)$$
- **Two-qubit gates** (e.g., Cross-Resonance, CZ) use either:
  - Direct capacitive coupling with controlled-frequency interaction, or
  - Microwave-activated schemes (cross-resonance), or
  - Tunable-coupler circuits (mediated by an auxiliary JJ).

#### 2.1.4 Readout

**Dispersive readout** via a readout resonator (coplanar waveguide resonator) is the standard technique. The qubit shifts the resonator frequency by $\chi$ (the dispersive shift) depending on its state, enabling a QND (Quantum Non-Demolition) measurement in the strong-dispersive regime:

$$\boxed{\chi = \frac{g^2}{\Delta} \alpha / (\hbar \Delta)}$$

where $g$ is the qubit–resonator coupling and $\Delta = \omega_q - \omega_r$ is the detuning.

---

### 2.2 Trapped Ions — Operational Principles

#### 2.2.1 The Paul Trap (RF Quadrupole Trap)

A single ion of mass $m$ and charge $e$ is confined by a time-varying quadrupole electric field. In the **pseudopotential approximation**, the secular motion in each direction $i \in \{x, y, z\}$ is harmonic with frequency:

$$\boxed{\omega_i = \frac{e V_{RF}}{m \Omega_{RF} r_0^2} \cdot q_i}$$

where:
- $V_{RF}$ is the peak RF voltage
- $\Omega_{RF}$ is the RF drive frequency
- $r_0$ is the trap electrode distance
- $q_i$ is the dimensionless Mathieu stability parameter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Paul traps work:** Earnshaw's theorem forbids static-electric-field confinement of charges. Paul solved this by using **time-averaged** (ponderomotive) potentials from an oscillating quadrupole field. The ion sits at the RF null where the field magnitude is minimum.

#### 2.2.2 Internal Qubit States

Common ion species and their qubit transitions:

| Ion | Qubit states | Transition | Wavelength |
|---|---|---|---|
| ⁴⁰Ca⁺ | 4S₁/₂ (m=−1/2, +1/2) | Zeeman | 729 nm |
| ¹⁷¹Yb⁺ | ²S₁/₂ (F=0, F=1) | Hyperfine | 12.6 GHz (microwave) |
| ⁸⁸Sr⁺ | 5S₁/₂ (m=−1/2, +1/2) | Zeeman | 674 nm |
| ¹³⁷Ba⁺ | ⁶S₁/₂ (F=2, F=3) | Hyperfine | 9.2 GHz (microwave) |
| ⁹Be⁺ | ²S₁/₂ (m=−3/2, +1/2) | Hyperfine | 1.25 GHz |

#### 2.2.3 Ion–Laser Interaction — The Lamb–Dicke Regime

When an ion is confined to a region much smaller than the laser wavelength ($x_{zp} \ll \lambda$), the **Lamb–Dicke parameter** is small:

$$\boxed{\eta = k \sqrt{\frac{\hbar}{2 m \omega}} = k x_{zp}}$$

where $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ is the laser wavenumber and $x_{zp} = \sqrt{\hbar/(2m\omega)}$ is the zero-point spread. In this regime, single-photon transitions cleanly address internal states without disturbing the motional mode — essential for high-fidelity operations.

#### 2.2.4 Cooling

- **Doppler cooling limit:** $T_D = \hbar \Gamma / 2 k_B$ (for ⁴⁰Ca⁺, ~ 0.5 mK).
- **Resolved sideband cooling:** pumps population to the motional ground state $\bar{n} \to 0$ by driving the red sideband $\vert g, n \rangle \to \vert e, n-1 \rangle$.

#### 2.2.5 Single-Qubit Gates

A resonant laser or microwave pulse drives Rabi oscillations between |g⟩ ↔ |e⟩. The rotation angle is $\theta = \Omega t$, where $\Omega$ is the Rabi frequency. Choosing $\theta = \pi, \pi/2$ implements X or Hadamard-like operations.

#### 2.2.6 Two-Qubit Gates — The Mølmer–Sørensen Gate

The **MS gate** is the workhorse entangling operation. It uses a *pair* of lasers (or a bichromatic beam) tuned near the *blue* and *red* sidebands of a shared vibrational mode of frequency $\omega_z$. After a gate time $T_g = 2\pi/\delta$ (where $\delta$ is the detuning from the sideband), the motional displacement returns to zero and the ions acquire an effective Ising-type interaction:

$$\boxed{H_{\text{MS}}^{\text{eff}} = \frac{\hbar \Omega^2 \eta^2}{2\delta}\sum_{i<j}\sigma_x^{(i)}\sigma_x^{(j)}}$$

This produces a maximally entangled state independent of the motional state of the ions — a key robustness feature.

#### 2.2.7 Readout

**Electron-shelving** (or "quantum jump") detection: A laser couples one qubit state to a rapidly-decaying excited state that scatters many photons. Detection of fluorescence ⇒ ion was in that state; absence of fluorescence ⇒ ion was in the other (dark) state. Typical fidelity: 99.9%+.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Hamiltonian of a JJ-Shunted Oscillator

**Starting point:** A Cooper-pair box is a superconducting island connected to a reservoir via a JJ of energy $E_J$ and total capacitance $C_\Sigma$. The island has gate-induced charge $Q_g = 2 e n_g$.

**Step 1 — Identify conjugate variables.** Let $\hat{\varphi}$ be the phase across the JJ and $\hat{n}$ the number of excess Cooper pairs on the island. The commutation relation is:

$$[\hat{\varphi}, \hat{n}] = i$$

This is the Cooper-pair analogue of $[\hat{x}, \hat{p}] = i\hbar$ with $\hat{n}$ playing the role of $\hat{p}/\hbar$.

**Step 2 — Write the charging energy.** The electrostatic energy stored when $n$ Cooper pairs sit on the island is:

$$U_{\text{ch}} = \frac{(2en)^2}{2 C_\Sigma} = 4 E_C \hat{n}^2$$

When a gate voltage $V_g$ is applied, an offset charge $n_g$ appears:

$$U_{\text{ch}} = 4 E_C (\hat{n} - n_g)^2$$

**Step 3 — Write the Josephson energy.** From the Josephson current–phase relation $I = I_c \sin\varphi$, integrating gives the energy stored in the JJ as a function of phase difference:

$$U_J = -E_J \cos(\varphi) \quad \text{where} \quad E_J = \frac{\hbar I_c}{2e}$$

**Step 4 — Form the full Hamiltonian.** Summing both contributions:

$$H = 4 E_C (\hat{n} - n_g)^2 - E_J \cos(\hat{\varphi})$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the duality.**
- If $E_C \gg E_J$: $\hat{n}$ is "stiff" — qubit lives in the **charge basis** (charge qubit / Cooper-pair box).
- If $E_J \gg E_C$: $\hat{\varphi}$ is "stiff" — qubit lives in the **phase basis** (transmon, flux qubit).

**Step 6 — Expand in the transmon limit.** For $E_J/E_C \gg 1$, expand $\cos\hat\varphi$ to 4th order (Duffing approximation):

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{transmon}} &= 4 E_C \hat{n}^2 - E_J \left(1 - \frac{\hat{\varphi}^2}{2} + \frac{\hat{\varphi}^4}{24} - \cdots\right) \\
&\approx \underbrace{4 E_C \hat{n}^2 + \frac{E_J}{2}\hat{\varphi}^2}_{\text{harmonic oscillator}} \;-\; \underbrace{\frac{E_J}{24}\hat{\varphi}^4}_{\text{anharmonicity}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The first bracketed terms form an oscillator of frequency:

$$\hbar \omega_q = \sqrt{8 E_C E_J}$$

The anharmonicity (level-spacing difference) evaluates to:

$$\alpha \approx -E_C$$

This **negative** anharmonicity is what makes the transmon anharmonic while keeping it charge-noise-insensitive.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Effective Hamiltonian of the Mølmer–Sørensen Gate

**Setup:** Two ions in a linear Paul trap share a common axial mode of frequency $\omega_z$. A bichromatic laser of frequencies $\omega_0 \pm (\omega_z + \delta)$ illuminates both ions, where $\omega_0$ is the qubit carrier frequency.

**Step 1 — Interaction picture Hamiltonian.** In the Lamb–Dicke regime and rotating frame, the ion–laser interaction for ion $j$ becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_j^{(I)}(t) &= \hbar \Omega \sigma_x^{(j)}\Big[\cos((\omega_z + \delta)t + \eta(a e^{-i\omega_z t} + a^\dagger e^{i\omega_z t})) \\
&\quad + \cos(-(\omega_z + \delta)t + \eta(a e^{-i\omega_z t} + a^\dagger e^{i\omega_z t}))\Big]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Magnus expansion (1st order).** The time-evolution operator at gate time $T_g = 2\pi/\delta$ is expanded in powers of $\eta$. Keeping only the leading term after eliminating the motion:

$$U(T_g) = \exp\left(-i \frac{\Omega^2 \eta^2}{2\delta} T_g \sum_{i<j} \sigma_x^{(i)}\sigma_x^{(j)}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Set gate time for a maximally entangling gate.** A fully entangling XX gate requires:

$$\frac{\Omega^2 \eta^2}{2\delta} \cdot \frac{2\pi}{\delta} = \frac{\pi}{4}$$

This determines $\delta$ (and hence the gate duration $T_g$):

$$\boxed{\delta = 2\Omega \eta \sqrt{\pi}}$$

**Step 4 — Effective Ising Hamiltonian.** Therefore the effective interaction is:

$$\boxed{H_{\text{MS}}^{\text{eff}} = \frac{\hbar \Omega^2 \eta^2}{2\delta} \sum_{i<j}\sigma_x^{(i)}\sigma_x^{(j)}}$$

This is a *geometric* gate: the ions' motional wave packets trace closed loops in phase space during the gate, returning to the origin. Motional-state errors are suppressed by choosing $T_g$ to be a multiple of the motional period.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Decay Processes

| Process | Superconducting Qubits | Trapped Ions |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy relaxation (T₁)** | Dielectric loss in oxides/substrates; quasi-particle tunneling; Purcell decay via readout resonator | Spontaneous emission from excited state; off-resonant scattering from trapping lasers |
| **Dephasing (T₂)** | Flux noise (1/f), charge noise (1/f), TLS defects, photon shot noise in readout | Magnetic field fluctuations, laser phase/intensity noise, motional heating |
| **Scaling limit** | Refrigerator cooling power (mK); 2D lithography | Laser beam addressing; motional-mode crowding beyond ~ 50 ions |
| **Mitigation** | Better dielectrics, tantalum capacitors, 3D cavities | Magic-wavelength trapping, dynamical decoupling, surface-electrode traps |

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Transmon Anharmonicity

**Problem:** A transmon qubit has $E_J/h = 10$ GHz and $E_C/h = 200$ MHz. Estimate the qubit frequency and the anharmonicity.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Qubit transition frequency:**

$$\omega_{01}/2\pi = \sqrt{8 (E_J/h)(E_C/h)} = \sqrt{8 \times 10\,\text{GHz} \times 0.2\,\text{GHz}}$$

$$= \sqrt{16}\,\text{GHz} = 4\,\text{GHz}$$

**Step 2 — Anharmonicity (approximate):**

$$\alpha/2\pi \approx -E_C/h = -200\,\text{MHz}$$

**Step 3 — Frequency of second transition:**

$$\omega_{12}/2\pi = \omega_{01}/2\pi + \alpha/2\pi = 4\,\text{GHz} - 0.2\,\text{GHz} = 3.8\,\text{GHz}$$

**Step 4 — Addressability.** A π-pulse on |0⟩ → |1⟩ at 4 GHz with bandwidth ≪ 200 MHz will not drive |1⟩ → |2⟩ — leakage is suppressed. **This is what makes the transmon a usable qubit.**

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Lamb–Dicke Parameter for ⁴⁰Ca⁺

**Problem:** A ⁴⁰Ca⁺ ion is trapped at axial frequency $\omega_z = 2\pi \times 1$ MHz. A 729 nm laser addresses the qubit transition. Compute $\eta$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Zero-point spread:**

$$x_{zp} = \sqrt{\frac{\hbar}{2 m \omega_z}}$$

with $m(^{40}\text{Ca}^+) = 40 \times 1.66 \times 10^{-27}\,\text{kg} = 6.64 \times 10^{-26}\,\text{kg}$.

$$x_{zp} = \sqrt{\frac{1.055 \times 10^{-34}}{2 \times 6.64 \times 10^{-26} \times 2\pi \times 10^6}} \approx 11.3\,\text{nm}$$

**Step 2 — Wavevector:**

$$k = \frac{2\pi}{729 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 8.62 \times 10^6\,\text{m}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Lamb–Dicke parameter:**

$$\eta = k \cdot x_{zp} = 8.62 \times 10^6 \times 11.3 \times 10^{-9} \approx 0.097$$

Since $\eta \approx 0.1 \ll 1$, the **Lamb–Dicke regime** holds: sideband transitions are well-resolved from the carrier, enabling clean quantum operations.

```mermaid
        L2 -- "alpha approx -200 MHz" --> L3
    end

    subgraph TI["TRAPPED ION HYPERFINE<br/>Energy Levels"]
        M0["F = 0  ground<br/>hyperfine qubit 0"]
        M1["F = 1  excited hyperfine<br/>qubit 1  delta f approx 12.6 GHz Yb 171"]
        M2["Optical P state<br/>Used for cooling and detection"]
        M3["Metastable D state<br/>Shelving level"]
        M0 -- "clock transition" --> M1
        M1 -- "laser 369 nm" --> M2
        M1 -- "411 nm" --> M3
    end
```

### 4.4 Gate Operation Sequence — Superconducting vs Trapped Ion

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User / Compiler
    participant E as Electronics / Laser
    participant Q as Qubit / Ion
    participant R as Readout

    Note over U,R: SUPERCONDUCTING (typical CZ gate)
    U->>E: Calibrate pulse (Gaussian, 40 ns, DRAG)
    E->>Q: Microwave π/2 on Q1
    E->>Q: Flux pulse on tunable coupler
    E->>Q: Microwave π/2 on Q1
    E->>R: Readout pulse to resonator
    R-->>U: Photon counts → classical bit

    Note over U,R: TRAPPED ION (Mølmer–Sørensen gate)
    U->>E: Set AOM frequency (blue + red sidebands)
    E->>Q: Bichromatic beam ON for Tg = 200 microseconds
    E->>Q: Optical pumping pulse (re-initialize)
    E->>R: Shelving laser + fluorescence detection
    R-->>U: Photon counts → classical bit
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use these diagrams:** These schematic flowcharts capture the key architectural and operational differences between the two platforms. Memorize them for KTU short-answer and diagram-labeling questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]** 
*What is a Josephson Junction? Explain how it provides the non-linearity needed for a superconducting qubit.* (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
A Josephson Junction (JJ) consists of two superconductors separated by a thin insulating barrier (≈ 1–2 nm). Cooper pairs tunnel coherently across the barrier. The current–phase relation is $I = I_c \sin\varphi$, giving a junction energy $E_J = \hbar I_c / 2e$ and an energy-phase relation $U = -E_J \cos\varphi$.
In an LC oscillator, all energy levels are equally spaced (harmonic), so a microwave pulse cannot selectively address just one transition. The JJ replaces the inductor with the **non-linear** $-E_J\cos\hat\varphi$ term, making the energy spectrum **anharmonic**. This allows isolation of the two lowest levels as the qubit |0⟩ and |1⟩. Thus the JJ is the essential element that turns a harmonic oscillator into a controllable two-level quantum system. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** 
*Why are trapped ion qubits called "atomic" qubits? List any two advantages they have over superconducting qubits.* (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
Trapped ion qubits use individual **charged atoms** (ions) such as ⁴⁰Ca⁺ or ¹⁷¹Yb⁺ held in vacuum by electromagnetic fields. The qubit is encoded in the ion's **internal electronic (or hyperfine) energy levels** — a property of the atom itself. Since all ions of a given isotope are *identical and reproducible by nature*, they are called "atomic qubits."
**Two advantages over superconducting qubits:**
1. **Long coherence times** — hyperfine clock transitions have T₂ > 1 second, while superconducting qubits have T₂ ≈ 100 μs.
2. **All-to-all connectivity** — the shared phonon bus mediates two-qubit gates between any pair of ions in the chain, whereas superconducting qubits are typically nearest-neighbor only.
*(Additional valid points: identical qubits by fabrication, narrow optical transitions, high gate fidelities > 99.9%.)* **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4 Choice A]** 
**(a)** With the help of a circuit diagram, describe the construction and working of a **transmon qubit**. Derive its Hamiltonian from the general Cooper-pair box Hamiltonian and show that the transmon regime is $E_J/E_C \gg 1$. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Explain the principle of **dispersive readout** of a transmon qubit using a coplanar waveguide resonator. Define the dispersive shift $\chi$ and state its order of magnitude. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Transmon Qubit Construction, Working, and Hamiltonian (7 marks)**

**Construction:** A transmon consists of two superconducting electrodes forming a capacitor of capacitance $C_\Sigma$ shunted by a Josephson Junction of energy $E_J$. The JJ is typically realized as an Al/AlOx/Al tunnel junction. The shunt capacitor may be a parallel-plate, finger, or large interdigitated design. The circuit is fabricated on a low-loss substrate (sapphire or high-resistivity Si).

**Working principle:** The JJ acts as a non-linear inductor, providing the $-E_J\cos\hat\varphi$ potential. The shunt capacitor supplies the quadratic charging energy $4E_C\hat{n}^2$. Together, the system forms a **quantum anharmonic oscillator**. By choosing $E_J/E_C \gg 1$, the device is biased deep in the phase regime where charge noise sensitivity becomes exponentially small.

**Derivation:**
The Hamiltonian of a generic Cooper-pair box is:

$$H = 4E_C(\hat{n} - n_g)^2 - E_J \cos\hat\varphi$$

The transmon is the *charge-insensitive* limit obtained by **increasing** $E_J/E_C$. Define the dimensionless parameter:

$$\lambda = \sqrt{8 E_C / E_J}$$

For $\lambda \ll 1$, expand $\hat{n}$ around its equilibrium value and quantize. The result is a **Duffing oscillator**:

$$H_{\text{transmon}} = \hbar\omega_q \hat{a}^\dagger \hat{a} + \frac{\hbar\alpha}{2}\hat{a}^\dagger\hat{a}^\dagger\hat{a}\hat{a}$$

with

$$\hbar\omega_q = \sqrt{8 E_J E_C} - E_C, \qquad \alpha \approx -E_C$$

**Charge noise suppression:** The energy-level dispersion with offset charge $n_g$ decays as $\exp(-\sqrt{8 E_J/E_C})$, justifying the regime $E_J/E_C \sim 50 - 200$ used in practice.

**Anharmonicity:** $|α| \sim 200$–$300$ MHz, enabling selective driving of the |0⟩ ↔ |1⟩ transition without leakage to |2⟩.

**Conclusion:** The transmon is engineered to balance two competing needs: large anharmonicity for qubit addressability and exponential insensitivity to charge noise for long coherence times. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Dispersive Readout (7 marks)**

**Setup:** Each transmon is capacitively coupled to a coplanar waveguide (CPW) resonator of frequency $\omega_r$, which serves as a readout "antenna." The coupling strength is $g$.

**Jaynes–Cummings Hamiltonian:** In the rotating-wave approximation:

$$H_{JC} = \hbar\omega_r \hat{a}^\dagger\hat{a} + \frac{\hbar\omega_q}{2}\hat\sigma_z + \hbar g(\hat\sigma_+ \hat{a} + \hat\sigma_- \hat{a}^\dagger)$$

**Dispersive regime:** When the detuning $|\Delta| = |\omega_q - \omega_r|$ exceeds $g$, second-order perturbation theory gives:

$$H_{\text{disp}} \approx \hbar\left(\omega_r + \frac{\sigma_z}{2}\chi\right)\hat{a}^\dagger\hat{a} + \frac{\hbar\omega_q}{2}\sigma_z$$

with

$$\chi = \frac{g^2 \alpha}{\Delta(\Delta - \alpha)}$$

**Readout principle:** The qubit state $\vert 0\rangle$ shifts the resonator frequency by $-\chi/2$; the $\vert 1\rangle$ state shifts it by $+\chi/2$. Sending a microwave probe at the bare frequency $\omega_r$ produces a transmitted/reflected signal whose phase (or amplitude) depends on the qubit state.

**Order of magnitude:** In practice, $\chi \sim 1$–$10$ MHz for transmon-resonator systems. The measurement is **QND** in the strong-dispersive limit: the qubit's $\sigma_z$ eigenstate is preserved while its value is determined with fidelity > 99%.

**Signal chain:** The reflected/transmitted signal is amplified by a TWPA (Travelling-Wave Parametric Amplifier) at 15 mK, then by a HEMT at 4 K, then digitized and demodulated by a FPGA. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4 Choice B]** 
**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **linear Paul trap** for confining charged ions. Derive the equation of motion and show how a stable secular frequency arises. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Describe the **Mølmer–Sørensen gate** for trapped ions. Explain why it is a *geometric* gate and how it produces an effective Ising interaction $\sigma_x^{(i)}\sigma_x^{(j)}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Linear Paul Trap (7 marks)**

**Construction:** Four hyperbolic electrodes arranged symmetrically around the trap axis produce an ideal quadrupole field. The end-caps (DC electrodes) provide axial confinement along $z$. The RF electrodes are driven by an oscillating voltage $V_{RF}\cos(\Omega_{RF} t)$.

**Ideal quadrupole potential:**

$$\Phi(x,y,t) = \frac{V_{RF}\cos(\Omega_{RF}t)}{2r_0^2}(x^2 - y^2)$$

Combined with the DC endcap potential $\Phi_{DC} = \kappa U_0 (z^2 - (x^2+y^2)/2)$, the equations of motion for an ion of charge $e$ and mass $m$ are:

$$\ddot{u}_i + \left[a_i + 2 q_i \cos(\Omega_{RF} t)\right]\frac{\Omega_{RF}^2}{4} u_i = 0$$

with dimensionless Mathieu parameters:

$$a_x = a_y = -\frac{a_z}{2} = \frac{4 e U_0 \kappa}{m \Omega_{RF}^2 r_0^2}, \qquad q_x = -q_y = \frac{2 e V_{RF}}{m \Omega_{RF}^2 r_0^2}, \qquad q_z = 0$$

**Solution — secular motion:** In the pseudopotential approximation, the rapid oscillation at $\Omega_{RF}$ averages to a slow harmonic motion with secular frequency:

$$\omega_i \approx \frac{q_i \Omega_{RF}}{2\sqrt{2}}$$

along each transverse axis, and $\omega_z = \sqrt{a_z}\Omega_{RF}/2$ axially.

**Stability condition:** Stable trapping requires the Mathieu parameters to lie within well-known stability regions of the $(a_i, q_i)$ plane — typically $|a_i|, |q_i| < 0.9$. In practice, $a \approx 0$ and $q \approx 0.2$–$0.4$.

**Working principle:** Although the time-averaged force vanishes at the trap center (Earnshaw), the time-averaged *kinetic energy* of the oscillating ion creates an effective harmonic potential (the "pseudopotential" or ponderomotive potential) that confines the ion.

**Conclusion:** Linear Paul traps confine individual ions in vacuum with secular frequencies in the 0.1–10 MHz range, with motional amplitudes of tens of nanometers — ideal for use as quantum memories and qubit hosts. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Mølmer–Sørensen Gate (7 marks)**

**Concept:** The MS gate entangles two (or more) ions using a bichromatic laser field tuned near the **blue** and **red** motional sidebands of a shared vibrational mode.

**Setup:** Let two ions share an axial mode of frequency $\omega_z$. Apply a bichromatic beam at frequencies $\omega_0 \pm (\omega_z + \delta)$ with $\delta \ll \omega_z$, where $\omega_0$ is the qubit carrier.

**Interaction Hamiltonian in the Lamb–Dicke regime:**

$$H_I(t) = \hbar\Omega\sum_{j=1,2}\sigma_x^{(j)}\left[e^{i(\omega_z+\delta)t} + e^{-i(\omega_z+\delta)t}\right]\left[\eta(a e^{-i\omega_z t} + a^\dagger e^{i\omega_z t})\right]$$

**Magnus expansion:** At gate time $T_g = 2\pi/\delta$, the motional displacement returns to zero for all initial motional states, leaving:

$$U(T_g) = \exp\left[-i\frac{\pi}{4}\sigma_x^{(1)}\sigma_x^{(2)}\right]$$

This is a maximally entangling $\pi/4$ rotation around the XX axis.

**Effective Ising Hamiltonian:** During the gate, the average Hamiltonian is:

$$H_{\text{MS}}^{\text{eff}} = \frac{\hbar\Omega^2\eta^2}{2\delta}\sigma_x^{(1)}\sigma_x^{(2)}$$

**Why geometric:** The motional displacement of each ion traces a *closed loop* in phase space, returning to the origin. The gate is therefore insensitive to the initial thermal motional state — a key robustness property.

**Gate time:** Typically $T_g = 100$–$500$ μs, much slower than superconducting gates but with much higher fidelity (> 99.9%).

**Conclusion:** The MS gate is the canonical two-qubit entangling operation in trapped-ion quantum computing, enabling universal quantum computation when combined with single-qubit rotations. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 4 Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Cooper-pair box and transmon regimes.** Students often write $E_J/E_C \ll 1$ when describing the transmon. The transmon requires $E_J/E_C \gg 1$. Examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the offset charge term.** When writing the CPB Hamiltonian, students omit $-n_g$ inside the square. The Hamiltonian is $4E_C(\hat{n}-n_g)^2$, not $4E_C\hat{n}^2$.
> 3. **Mixing up charge and flux qubit descriptions.** The flux qubit uses a SQUID loop with external flux bias; the charge qubit uses a single JJ with gate bias. Be precise.
> 4. **Stating Earnshaw's theorem incorrectly.** It forbids *static* charge confinement — not oscillating fields. State explicitly: "static electric fields cannot trap charges; time-varying (RF) fields can."
> 5. **Writing MS gate as $\sigma_z \sigma_z$ instead of $\sigma_x \sigma_x$.** The bichromatic beam drives an $\sigma_x$ coupling; the resulting two-qubit gate is along XX, not ZZ. (ZZ gates can be obtained by single-qubit rotations before/after.)
> 6. **Missing units or order-of-magnitude justifications.** Always state typical values: e.g., "$\chi \sim$ MHz", "$\eta \sim 0.1$", "$E_J/E_C \sim 50$–$200$".
> 7. **Omitting the cooling step in trapped-ion protocols.** A complete ion-trap protocol must include Doppler and (optionally) sideband cooling before gate operations. 1 mark lost if absent.
> 8. **Drawing wrong circuit for the transmon.** The transmon is a JJ in parallel with a *shunt capacitor*, NOT a JJ in series with an LC tank. Examiner deducts 1 mark for incorrect topology.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- **Superconducting Qubits (Quick-Revision Checklist):**
  - [ ] Built from **Josephson Junctions + capacitors** at ~ 15 mK.
  - [ ] **Transmon**: $E_J/E_C \sim 50$–$200$, exponentially charge-noise insensitive, anharmonicity $\alpha \sim -200$ MHz.
  - [ ] **Charge qubit (CPB)**: $E_C \gg E_J$, sensitive to charge noise, mostly of historical interest.
  - [ ] **Flux qubit**: Persistent-current states in a SQUID loop, tunable by external flux.
  - [ ] **Qubit frequency**: $f_{01} \sim 3$–$10$ GHz (microwave).
  - [ ] **Single-qubit gates**: Microwave XY pulses, 10–50 ns.
  - [ ] **Two-qubit gates**: Cross-resonance, CZ with tunable coupler.
  - [ ] **Readout**: Dispersive, via CPW resonator, $\chi \sim$ MHz.
  - [ ] **Coherence**: $T_1, T_2 \sim 50$–$300$ μs.
  - [ ] **Companies**: IBM, Google, Rigetti, IBM Quantum, OQC.

- **Trapped Ions (Quick-Revision Checklist):**
  - [ ] Confined in **Paul traps** (RF quadrupole + DC end-caps).
  - [ ] Qubit in **internal hyperfine / Zeeman states** (identical for every ion!).
  - [ ] **Lamb–Dicke regime**: $\eta = k x_{zp} \ll 1$.
  - [ ] **Cooling**: Doppler → resolved sideband cooling → $\bar n \to 0$.
  - [ ] **Single-qubit gates**: Resonant laser / microwave Rabi rotations.
  - [ ] **Two-qubit gates**: Mølmer–Sørensen (bichromatic, geometric, XX).
  - [ ] **Readout**: Electron shelving + fluorescence (PMT / camera).
  - [ ] **Coherence**: $T_2 > 1$ s (hyperfine clock transitions).
  - [ ] **Connectivity**: All-to-all via shared phonon mode.
  - [ ] **Companies**: IonQ, Quantinuum (Honeywell + Cambridge Quantum), Oxford Ionics.

- **Cross-Platform Key Differences:**
  - [ ] Temperature: SC @ mK; Ions @ room-temperature vacuum.
  - [ ] Speed: SC ~ ns; Ions ~ μs.
  - [ ] Coherence: Ions win by 4 orders of magnitude.
  - [ ] Connectivity: Ions all-to-all; SC nearest-neighbor.
  - [ ] Mass production: SC lithography wins; Ions are identical atoms.

- **Top 5 Must-Know Equations:**
  1. $E_J = \Phi_0 I_c / 2\pi$
  2. $H_{\text{CPB}} = 4E_C(\hat{n}-n_g)^2 - E_J\cos\hat\varphi$
  3. Transmon: $\hbar\omega_q = \sqrt{8E_J E_C}$, $\alpha \approx -E_C$.
  4. Lamb–Dicke: $\eta = k\sqrt{\hbar/(2m\omega)}$.
  5. MS gate: $H_{\text{eff}} = (\hbar\Omega^2\eta^2/2\delta)\sum_{i<j}\sigma_x^{(i)}\sigma_x^{(j)}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Strategy Tip:** In KTU 14-mark questions, always (i) state the regime/assumptions, (ii) write the Hamiltonian, (iii) derive the key expression with explicit steps, (iv) give a numerical estimate with units, and (v) discuss one limitation. This 5-part structure is what examiners reward.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quantum Machine Learning and Deep Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET863 - QUANTUM COMPUTING WITH PYTHON
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Quantum Machine Learning and Deep Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Quantum Machine Learning (QML) — Formal Definition

**Quantum Machine Learning (QML)** is an interdisciplinary research domain that investigates how principles from quantum mechanics — primarily **superposition**, **entanglement**, and **quantum interference** — can be systematically integrated with classical machine learning algorithms to achieve computational speedups, improved generalization, and novel representational capacities for high-dimensional data.

In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme definition, QML sits at the **intersection of three pillars**:

$$
\text{QML} \;=\; f(\text{Quantum Information Theory},\; \text{Classical ML},\; \text{Linear Algebra over Hilbert Spaces})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEIET863 — Module 4):**
> QML is *not* a replacement for classical ML. Instead, it uses **quantum subroutines** (e.g., quantum feature maps, variational circuits, Grover search) to accelerate specific algebraic sub-problems (matrix inversion, inner products, optimization) that bottleneck classical ML pipelines.

## 1.2 Quantum Deep Learning (QDL) — Formal Definition

**Quantum Deep Learning (QDL)** refers to the class of QML models that employ **deep parameterized quantum circuits** — also called **Parameterized Quantum Circuits (PQCs)** or **Variational Quantum Circuits (VQCs)** — to approximate highly non-linear functions. They are the quantum analog of classical Deep Neural Networks (DNNs), but where each "neuron" is replaced by a **quantum gate with tunable rotation parameters**.

$$
\text{QDL} \;\equiv\; \text{Stacked PQCs with hierarchical feature extraction}
$$

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Quantum Judge":**
> Imagine a courtroom where a judge must classify thousands of legal documents into "relevant" or "irrelevant". A *classical* judge reads each document one at a time. A *quantum machine learning* judge uses **Schrödinger's superposed docket** — she holds all documents in a *superposition* of "read" and "not read" states simultaneously, then uses **entanglement** to link related cases and **interference** to amplify the relevant patterns. A *quantum deep learning* judge is even smarter: she stacks multiple quantum reasoning layers (like a deep court hierarchy) — first layer identifies *keywords*, second identifies *themes*, third identifies *case precedents* — each layer built from tunable quantum gates. The verdict (measurement) collapses the wavefunction into a single, highly informed classification.

## 1.4 Key Metrics and Constants

- **Hilbert space dimension** for $n$ qubits: $\dim(\mathcal{H}) = 2^n$
- **Variational parameters** in a PQC: $\boldsymbol{\theta} = (\theta_1, \theta_2, \dots, \theta_m) \in \mathbb{R}^m$
- **Standard learning rate** in QML hybrid training: $\eta \in [0.001,\, 0.1]$
- **Quantum volume** (IBM benchmark): $V_Q = 2^{\min(n_{\text{qubits}},\, d_{\text{circuit depth}})}$
- **Fidelity** of quantum state preparation: $F(\rho, \sigma) = \left(\text{Tr}\sqrt{\sqrt{\rho}\,\sigma\sqrt{\rho}}\right)^2 \in [0, 1]$

## 1.5 The Three Paradigms of QML

| Paradigm | Data Encoding | Computation | Use-Case |
|----------|--------------|-------------|----------|
| **CC** — Classical-to-Classical | Classical | Classical ML enhanced by quantum kernels | Kernel SVMs, feature maps |
| **CQ** — Classical-to-Quantum | Classical | Quantum model (e.g., VQC) | Variational classifiers, QNNs |
| **QC** — Quantum-to-Classical | Quantum | Classical ML on quantum measurements | Quantum state classification |
| **QQ** — Quantum-to-Quantum | Quantum | Quantum | Theoretical end-goal (fault-tolerant era) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Architecture of a Variational Quantum Circuit (VQC)

A VQC — the workhorse of modern QML — consists of three structural blocks executed sequentially on a quantum processor:

1. **State Preparation (Feature Encoding / Embedding)**
   - Classical input vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is mapped into an $n$-qubit quantum state via a **feature map** $\mathcal{U}_\phi(\mathbf{x})$.
   - Common encodings: **Basis encoding**, **Amplitude encoding**, **Angle encoding**, **IQP encoding**.

2. **Parameterized Ansatz (Trainable Layers)**
   - A sequence of rotation and entangling gates with tunable parameters $\boldsymbol{\theta}$.
   - Acts as the *quantum analog* of weight matrices in classical DNNs.

3. **Measurement & Cost Function Evaluation**
   - Measurement operators (e.g., Pauli-Z on each qubit) yield expectation values.
   - A cost function $\mathcal{C}(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ aggregates these into a scalar loss.

The full forward pass is:

$$
\vert \psi(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\theta}) \rangle \;=\; U(\boldsymbol{\theta})\,\mathcal{U}_\phi(\mathbf{x})\,\vert 0 \rangle^{\otimes n}
$$

## 2.2 Quantum Feature Maps — The "Embedding" Step

A **quantum feature map** is a unitary operator $\mathcal{U}_\phi(\mathbf{x})$ that maps classical data into a quantum Hilbert space, ideally into a space that is **hard to simulate classically**.

The most general form (Havlicek et al., 2019):

$$
\mathcal{U}_\phi(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; \exp\!\left(i \sum_{S \subseteq [n]} \phi_S(\mathbf{x})\, \prod_{i \in S} P_i \right)
$$

where $P_i \in \{I, X, Y, Z\}$ are Pauli operators, and $\phi_S(\mathbf{x})$ is a data-dependent phase function.

## 2.3 Quantum Kernels — The Bridge to Classical ML

The **quantum kernel** between two data points $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{x}'$ is defined as the squared overlap of their feature-mapped states:

$$
K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') \;=\; \vert \langle \psi(\mathbf{x}) \vert \psi(\mathbf{x}') \rangle \vert^2
$$

This kernel is then plugged into a classical **Support Vector Machine (SVM)**:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i\, y_i\, K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}_i) \;+\; b
$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---------|---------|---------|
| 1 | Qubit state | $\vert \psi \rangle = \alpha\vert 0 \rangle + \beta\vert 1 \rangle$, $\vert\alpha\vert^2 + \vert\beta\vert^2 = 1$ | Superposition |
| 2 | Hilbert space dim. | $\dim(\mathcal{H}) = 2^n$ | For $n$ qubits |
| 3 | Feature map | $\mathcal{U}_\phi(\mathbf{x}) = \exp(i\sum_S \phi_S \prod_i P_i)$ | Data encoding |
| 4 | Quantum kernel | $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = \vert \langle \psi(\mathbf{x}) \vert \psi(\mathbf{x}') \rangle \vert^2$ | Inner product in Hilbert space |
| 5 | Expectation value | $\langle O \rangle = \langle \psi \vert O \vert \psi \rangle$ | Measurement outcome |
| 6 | PQC ansatz | $U(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \prod_{l=1}^{L} U_l(\theta_l)\, V_l$ | Trainable layers |
| 7 | Cost function | $\mathcal{C}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ \langle \psi_i(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \vert H \vert \psi_i(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \rangle - y_i \right]^2$ | Loss to minimize |
| 8 | Parameter-shift rule | $\partial_{\theta_k} \langle O \rangle = \frac{1}{2}\left[\langle O \rangle_{\theta_k + \pi/2} - \langle O \rangle_{\theta_k - \pi/2}\right]$ | Exact gradient (no backprop needed) |
| 9 | Variational update | $\theta_k^{(t+1)} = \theta_k^{(t)} - \eta\, \partial_{\theta_k} \mathcal{C}$ | Gradient descent |
| 10 | Quantum fidelity | $F(\rho, \sigma) = \left(\text{Tr}\sqrt{\sqrt{\rho}\sigma\sqrt{\rho}}\right)^2$ | State similarity |
| 11 | Entangling gate (CNOT) | $\text{CNOT}\vert a,b \rangle = \vert a, a \oplus b \rangle$ | Creates entanglement |
| 12 | Rotational gate | $R_y(\theta) = e^{-i\theta Y/2}$ | Trainable parameter |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Quantum Kernel Methods**: Drug discovery (molecular fingerprint classification)
- **Variational Quantum Eigensolver (VQE)**: Materials science, chemistry ground-state energy
- **Quantum Convolutional Neural Networks (QCNN)**: Image classification on near-term hardware
- **Quantum GANs (QGAN)**: Quantum data generation, quantum state tomography
- **Hybrid Quantum-Classical Loops**: Finance (portfolio optimization), logistics (route planning)

## 2.6 The Parameter-Shift Rule — A Critical KTU Concept

Since quantum hardware cannot perform backpropagation through a measurement, the **parameter-shift rule** provides an *exact* gradient by evaluating the circuit at two shifted parameter values:

$$
\frac{\partial \langle O \rangle_\theta}{\partial \theta} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\left[\langle O \rangle_{\theta + \pi/2} - \langle O \rangle_{\theta - \pi/2}\right]
$$

This requires **two circuit evaluations per parameter** — a 2-fold cost over classical backprop, but enables gradient-based training on actual quantum hardware.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Expectation Value of a Pauli-Z Measurement on a Qubit

We begin with the simplest case: a single-qubit ansatz $R_y(\theta)\vert 0 \rangle$ and observable $Z$.

**Step 1:** Apply the rotation gate to the initial state $\vert 0 \rangle$.

$$
R_y(\theta)\vert 0 \rangle \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} \cos(\theta/2) & -\sin(\theta/2) \\ \sin(\theta/2) & \cos(\theta/2) \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} \cos(\theta/2) \\ \sin(\theta/2) \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 2:** Write the resulting state vector.

$$
\vert \psi(\theta) \rangle \;=\; \cos(\theta/2)\vert 0 \rangle \;+\; \sin(\theta/2)\vert 1 \rangle
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the expectation value of the Pauli-Z operator $Z = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$.

$$
\langle Z \rangle_\theta \;=\; \langle \psi(\theta) \vert Z \vert \psi(\theta) \rangle
$$

**Step 4:** Expand using matrix multiplication.

$$
Z \vert \psi(\theta) \rangle \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} \cos(\theta/2) \\ \sin(\theta/2) \end{pmatrix} \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} \cos(\theta/2) \\ -\sin(\theta/2) \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the inner product with the bra $\langle \psi(\theta) \vert = (\cos(\theta/2),\,\sin(\theta/2))$.

$$
\langle Z \rangle_\theta \;=\; \cos(\theta/2) \cdot \cos(\theta/2) \;+\; \sin(\theta/2) \cdot (-\sin(\theta/2)) \;=\; \cos^2(\theta/2) - \sin^2(\theta/2)
$$

**Step 6:** Apply the trigonometric double-angle identity $\cos^2(x) - \sin^2(x) = \cos(2x)$ with $x = \theta/2$.

$$
\langle Z \rangle_\theta \;=\; \cos(\theta)
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\langle Z \rangle_\theta \;=\; \cos(\theta)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This identity is the **single most important derivation** for KTU Part B questions on QML. A parameterized qubit with $R_y(\theta)$ produces a measurement outcome of $\cos(\theta)$ on the computational basis — the *exact* sinusoidal non-linearity that quantum circuits exploit to model continuous functions.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Parameter-Shift Rule

We now prove the parameter-shift rule for a gate $G(\theta) = e^{-i\theta P/2}$ where $P^2 = I$ (e.g., $P = X, Y, Z$).

**Step 1:** Express the expectation value of an observable $O$ as a function of $\theta$.

$$
\langle O \rangle_\theta \;=\; \langle 0 \vert G^\dagger(\theta)\, O\, G(\theta) \vert 0 \rangle
$$

**Step 2:** Use the spectral decomposition $G(\theta) = \cos(\theta/2) I - i\sin(\theta/2) P$.

**Step 3:** Compute $G^\dagger(\theta) O G(\theta)$ by expanding. Since $P^2 = I$, terms simplify to two spectral projections.

**Step 4:** After expansion (using $\cos^2 + \sin^2 = 1$ identities), the result separates into a constant part and a part proportional to $\sin(\theta)$ or $\cos(\theta)$:

$$
\langle O \rangle_\theta \;=\; A + B \sin(\theta) + C \cos(\theta)
$$

**Step 5:** Differentiate with respect to $\theta$.

$$
\frac{\partial \langle O \rangle_\theta}{\partial \theta} \;=\; B \cos(\theta) - C \sin(\theta)
$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate at $\theta + s$ and $\theta - s$ and subtract. Choosing $s = \pi/2$:

$$
\langle O \rangle_{\theta + \pi/2} - \langle O \rangle_{\theta - \pi/2} \;=\; 2B \cos(\theta) - 2C \sin(\theta) \;=\; 2\,\frac{\partial \langle O \rangle_\theta}{\partial \theta}
$$

**Step 7:** Rearrange.

$$
\frac{\partial \langle O \rangle_\theta}{\partial \theta} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\left[\langle O \rangle_{\theta + \pi/2} - \langle O \rangle_{\theta - \pi/2}\right]
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\frac{\partial \langle O \rangle_\theta}{\partial \theta} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\left[\langle O \rangle_{\theta + \pi/2} - \langle O \rangle_{\theta - \pi/2}\right]}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance for KTU:** This is the **backpropagation replacement** for quantum circuits. It is hardware-friendly, exact (not approximate), and applies to *any* parameterized gate whose generator squares to identity.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Quantum Kernel Inner Product

Given two data points $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}'$ mapped by feature map $\mathcal{U}_\phi$:

**Step 1:** Define the kernel as the overlap.

$$
K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') \;=\; \vert \langle 0 \vert \mathcal{U}_\phi^\dagger(\mathbf{x}')\, \mathcal{U}_\phi(\mathbf{x}) \vert 0 \rangle \vert^2
$$

**Step 2:** Recognize that the inner product $\langle \psi(\mathbf{x}') \vert \psi(\mathbf{x}) \rangle$ is a complex number in general.

**Step 3:** For *Havlicek-style* feature maps (where the encoding is Hermitian-preserving), the inner product is real and non-negative, so the absolute value is redundant.

**Step 4:** Substitute the IQP feature map $\mathcal{U}_\phi(\mathbf{x}) = \exp(i\sum_{S} x_S \prod_{i \in S} Z_i)$ (using $x_S = \prod_{i \in S} x_i$ for polynomial features).

**Step 5:** Using the Hadamard test or swap test, the kernel is estimated as the probability of measuring the ancilla qubit in $\vert 0 \rangle$:

$$
K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\left[1 + \langle 0 \vert \mathcal{U}_\phi^\dagger(\mathbf{x}')\, \mathcal{U}_\phi(\mathbf{x}) \vert 0 \rangle \right]
$$

**Step 6:** For the polynomial feature map with degree-2 features, the kernel decomposes as:

$$
K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') \;=\; \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2}\vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}' \vert^2 \right) \cdot \prod_{i < j} \exp\!\left((x_i x_j)(x_i' x_j') \right)
$$

**Step 7:** This is a **classical kernel that is provably hard to compute classically for high-dimensional data** — a key motivation for quantum advantage.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of QML and QDL

| Property | Classical DNN | QML (Shallow) | QDL (Deep PQC) |
|----------|-------------|---------------|-----------------|
| Basic unit | Neuron (scalar) | Qubit (2-dim) | Qubit + entanglement |
| Trainable params | Weights $W$, biases $b$ | Rotation angles $\theta$ | Angles + entangling topology |
| Non-linearity | Activation (ReLU, sigmoid) | Measurement collapse | Sinusoidal $\cos(\theta)$ |
| Backprop | Yes (autodiff) | N/A (use parameter-shift) | N/A (use parameter-shift) |
| Expressibility | Polynomial in width | Polynomial in circuit depth | Exponential in qubits (theoretical) |
| Noise tolerance | High | Low (NISQ-limited) | Low (NISQ-limited) |
| Hardware | GPU/TPU | Superconducting/Photonic | Superconducting/Photonic |
| Gradient cost | $O(P)$ (one backward pass) | $O(2P)$ (two forward passes) | $O(2P)$ per layer |

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Variational Quantum Classifier (VQC) Decision Function

For binary classification with label $y \in \{0, 1\}$:

**Step 1:** Encode input $\mathbf{x}$ via feature map $\mathcal{U}_\phi(\mathbf{x})$ into $n$-qubit state.

**Step 2:** Apply trainable ansatz $U(\boldsymbol{\theta})$.

**Step 3:** Measure expectation of $Z$ on the first qubit (or any designated readout qubit).

**Step 4:** Map the expectation to a probability via a sigmoid:

$$
P(y = 1 \vert \mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\theta}) \;=\; \sigma\!\left(\langle Z_1 \rangle_{\mathbf{x},\boldsymbol{\theta}}\right) \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\langle Z_1 \rangle}}
$$

**Step 5:** The decision boundary is the contour where $P = 0.5$, i.e., $\langle Z_1 \rangle = 0$.

**Step 6:** The cost is binary cross-entropy:

$$
\mathcal{C}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \;=\; -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \log \hat{y}_i + (1 - y_i) \log(1 - \hat{y}_i) \right]
$$

where $\hat{y}_i = \sigma(\langle Z_1 \rangle_{\mathbf{x}_i, \boldsymbol{\theta}})$.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Hybrid Quantum-Classical Training Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Classical Dataset X, y] --> B[Feature Map U_phi of x]
    B --> C[Initialize Parameters theta]
    C --> D[Quantum Circuit on QPU]
    D --> E[Measurement Expectation value]
    E --> F[Classical Cost Function C of theta]
    F --> G{Converged?}
    G -- No --> H[Compute Gradient via Parameter-Shift Rule]
    H --> I[Classical Optimizer Adam, COBYLA, SPSA]
    I --> J[Update theta = theta minus eta times gradient]
    J --> D
    G -- Yes --> K[Trained PQC Model]
    K --> L[Deployment Predict on New Data]
```

## 4.2 Anatomy of a Variational Quantum Circuit (VQC)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SP[State Preparation - Feature Encoding]
        SP1[Classical x] --> SP2[Angle Encoding R_x x]
        SP2 --> SP3[Entangling Layer CNOTs]
    end
    subgraph AN[Parameterized Ansatz - Trainable]
        AN1[Rotation Layer R_y theta 1] --> AN2[Entangling Layer]
        AN2 --> AN3[Rotation Layer R_y theta L]
    end
    subgraph MS[Measurement and Readout]
        MS1[Pauli Z expectation on each qubit] --> MS2[Cost Function Aggregation]
        MS2 --> MS3[Output probability or class label]
    end
    SP3 --> AN1
    AN3 --> MS1
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TOP[QML Taxonomy]
        A1[Quantum Kernel Methods] --> A1A[SVM with Quantum Kernel]
        A1 --> A1B[Quantum KPCA]
        A2[Variational Quantum Models] --> A2A[VQC Classifier]
        A2 --> A2B[QNN Regressor]
        A2 --> A2C[QCNN for Images]
        A3[Quantum Generative Models] --> A3A[QGAN]
        A3 --> A3B[Boltzmann Machines]

        A4[Quantum Reinforcement Learning] --> A4A[Policy Gradient with VQC]
        A4 --> A4B[Quantum Actor Critic]
    end
```

## 4.4 Quantum Neural Network (QNN) Layer-by-Layer Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    I0[Input x1 x2 x3] --> L1[Qubit 1 Rotation R_x]
    I0 --> L2[Qubit 2 Rotation R_x]
    I0 --> L3[Qubit 3 Rotation R_x]
    L1 --> E1[CNOT 1 to 2]
    L2 --> E1
    L2 --> E2[CNOT 2 to 3]
    L3 --> E2
    E1 --> P1[Trainable R_y theta 11]
    E2 --> P2[Trainable R_y theta 22]
    P1 --> M1[Measure Z on Qubit 1]
    P2 --> M2[Measure Z on Qubit 2]
    M1 --> OUT[Output layer]
    M2 --> OUT
```

## 4.5 Quantum Deep Learning (QDL) — Parameter-Shift Training Loop

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CL as Classical CPU
    participant QPU as Quantum Processor
    CL ->> QPU: Send circuit with theta
    QPU ->> CL: Return expectation of O at theta
    CL ->> QPU: Send circuit with theta plus pi/2
    QPU ->> CL: Return expectation at shifted theta
    CL ->> QPU: Send circuit with theta minus pi/2
    QPU ->> CL: Return expectation at shifted theta
    CL ->> CL: Compute gradient using parameter shift rule
    CL ->> CL: Update theta via optimizer
    CL ->> QPU: Send circuit with new theta
    QPU ->> CL: Return new expectation
    CL ->> CL: Check convergence
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Quantum Machine Learning (QML). List any two advantages of using quantum feature maps over classical kernels.**

**Model Answer:**

Quantum Machine Learning (QML) is an interdisciplinary field that combines quantum computing with classical machine learning by using quantum mechanical principles such as **superposition** and **entanglement** to represent, process, and learn from data in exponentially large Hilbert spaces.

**Two advantages of quantum feature maps:**

1. **Exponential feature space:** A quantum feature map on $n$ qubits implicitly operates in a $2^n$-dimensional Hilbert space, allowing complex feature combinations that would be intractable for classical kernels.

2. **Hard-to-simulate kernels:** Certain feature maps (e.g., IQP circuits) produce kernels that are *provably* hard to compute classically, suggesting potential quantum advantage in supervised learning tasks.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for advantages)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the parameter-shift rule? Why is it essential for training Variational Quantum Circuits?**

**Model Answer:**

The **parameter-shift rule** is a method to compute the exact gradient of a quantum circuit's expectation value with respect to a variational parameter $\theta_k$:

$$
\frac{\partial \langle O \rangle}{\partial \theta_k} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\left[\langle O \rangle_{\theta_k + \pi/2} - \langle O \rangle_{\theta_k - \pi/2}\right]
$$

**Why it is essential:**

- Quantum hardware cannot perform classical backpropagation through measurement.
- The rule provides an **exact** (not approximate) gradient using only two additional circuit evaluations.
- It applies to *any* parameterized gate of the form $G(\theta) = e^{-i\theta P/2}$ where $P^2 = I$, making it hardware-agnostic and suitable for NISQ devices.

*(3 marks: 1 for rule, 2 for justification)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Variational Quantum Circuit (VQC) used in Quantum Machine Learning. Discuss the role of each of the three stages: feature encoding, parameterized ansatz, and measurement.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a single-qubit circuit where the state is prepared as $\vert \psi(\theta) \rangle = R_y(\theta)\vert 0 \rangle$, derive the expectation value $\langle Z \rangle$ as a function of $\theta$. Hence, explain how this $\cos(\theta)$ dependence provides a non-linearity for quantum models.

#### Model Solution for (a):

A **Variational Quantum Circuit (VQC)** consists of three sequential stages executed on a quantum processor:

**Stage 1 — Feature Encoding (State Preparation):**
Classical data $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is encoded into a quantum state via a unitary feature map:

$$
\mathcal{U}_\phi(\mathbf{x})\vert 0 \rangle^{\otimes n} \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{2^n - 1} c_k(\mathbf{x}) \vert k \rangle
$$

For example, angle encoding sets qubit rotations to data values: $R_x(x_i)\vert 0 \rangle$ for each qubit $i$.

**Stage 2 — Parameterized Ansatz:**
A trainable unitary $U(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \prod_{l=1}^{L} U_l(\boldsymbol{\theta}_l) V_l$ composed of alternating rotation and entangling layers. The rotation layers contain tunable parameters $\theta_i$, and the entangling layers (CNOTs) generate correlations between qubits.

**Stage 3 — Measurement:**
Pauli operators (typically $Z$) are measured on designated qubits. The expectation value is:

$$
\langle O \rangle_{\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\theta}} \;=\; \langle 0 \vert \mathcal{U}_\phi^\dagger(\mathbf{x})\, U^\dagger(\boldsymbol{\theta})\, O\, U(\boldsymbol{\theta})\, \mathcal{U}_\phi(\mathbf{x}) \vert 0 \rangle
$$

The role of each stage:

| Stage | Role | Classical Analog |
|-------|------|------------------|
| Encoding | Map data to Hilbert space | Input layer |
| Ansatz | Apply trainable transformation | Hidden layers |
| Measurement | Extract scalar output | Output layer |

*(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for stage descriptions, 2 for roles)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1:** Apply the $R_y(\theta)$ gate to $\vert 0 \rangle$.

$$
R_y(\theta) \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} \cos(\theta/2) & -\sin(\theta/2) \\ \sin(\theta/2) & \cos(\theta/2) \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
R_y(\theta)\vert 0 \rangle \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} \cos(\theta/2) \\ \sin(\theta/2) \end{pmatrix} \;=\; \cos(\theta/2)\vert 0 \rangle + \sin(\theta/2)\vert 1 \rangle
$$

**Step 2:** Write $Z = \text{diag}(1, -1)$ and compute $Z\vert \psi \rangle$.

$$
Z \vert \psi \rangle \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} \cos(\theta/2) \\ -\sin(\theta/2) \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the inner product.

$$
\langle Z \rangle \;=\; \cos^2(\theta/2) - \sin^2(\theta/2)
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the double-angle identity.

$$
\cos^2(x) - \sin^2(x) \;=\; \cos(2x), \quad x = \theta/2
$$

$$
\boxed{\langle Z \rangle_\theta \;=\; \cos(\theta)}
$$

**Discussion of non-linearity:**
The $\cos(\theta)$ dependence introduces a *bounded, periodic, and non-polynomial* non-linearity into the model. Unlike polynomial activations in classical networks (e.g., ReLU), the quantum model naturally produces a sinusoidal response to parameter changes, which can be tuned to approximate any continuous function on a compact domain (by the **Universal Approximation Theorem for PQCs**). Moreover, entangling layers extend this to multi-variate non-linearities across qubits.

*(7 marks: 4 for derivation, 3 for non-linearity discussion)*

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define a quantum kernel. Show that the quantum kernel between two data points $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{x}'$ under feature map $\mathcal{U}_\phi$ is given by $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = \vert \langle 0 \vert \mathcal{U}_\phi^\dagger(\mathbf{x}')\mathcal{U}_\phi(\mathbf{x}) \vert 0 \rangle \vert^2$. Explain how this kernel is integrated with a classical SVM.

**(b) [7 Marks]** State and prove the parameter-shift rule for a parameterized gate $G(\theta) = e^{-i\theta P/2}$ with $P^2 = I$. Show that the gradient can be computed exactly using two circuit evaluations.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition:** A **quantum kernel** is a similarity function between two data points, computed as the squared fidelity of their quantum-encoded representations.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Let $\vert \psi(\mathbf{x}) \rangle = \mathcal{U}_\phi(\mathbf{x}) \vert 0 \rangle^{\otimes n}$ be the encoded state for $\mathbf{x}$.

**Step 2:** The inner product (overlap) between two such states is:

$$
\langle \psi(\mathbf{x}') \vert \psi(\mathbf{x}) \rangle \;=\; \langle 0 \vert \mathcal{U}_\phi^\dagger(\mathbf{x}')\, \mathcal{U}_\phi(\mathbf{x}) \vert 0 \rangle
$$

**Step 3:** Since this complex number may have a phase, we take the squared modulus to obtain a real, non-negative kernel:

$$
K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') \;=\; \big\vert \langle 0 \vert \mathcal{U}_\phi^\dagger(\mathbf{x}')\, \mathcal{U}_\phi(\mathbf{x}) \vert 0 \rangle \big\vert^2
$$

**Step 4:** Expanding the modulus: $\vert z \vert^2 = z^* z$, with $z = \langle 0 \vert \mathcal{U}_\phi^\dagger(\mathbf{x}')\, \mathcal{U}_\phi(\mathbf{x}) \vert 0 \rangle$.

**Step 5:** For Hermitian-preserving feature maps (such as IQP encodings), $z$ is real, so $\vert z \vert^2 = z^2$.

**Integration with Classical SVM:**

In a **Quantum Kernel SVM**, the kernel matrix $\mathbf{K}_{ij} = K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ is computed (either directly on a quantum computer via the Hadamard test, or simulated classically for small systems) and fed into the classical SVM dual optimization:

$$
\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \sum_i \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)
$$

subject to $0 \le \alpha_i \le C$ and $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$.

The decision function becomes:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; \text{sign}\!\left( \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}_i) + b \right)
$$

*(7 marks: 2 for definition, 3 for derivation, 2 for SVM integration)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Statement:** For a parameterized gate $G(\theta) = e^{-i\theta P/2}$ where $P$ is a Pauli matrix (so $P^2 = I$), the gradient of an expectation value $\langle O \rangle_\theta$ is:

$$
\frac{\partial \langle O \rangle_\theta}{\partial \theta} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\left[ \langle O \rangle_{\theta + \pi/2} - \langle O \rangle_{\theta - \pi/2} \right]
$$

**Proof:**

**Step 1:** Spectral decomposition of $G(\theta)$:

$$
G(\theta) \;=\; \cos(\theta/2) I - i \sin(\theta/2) P
$$

This is valid because $P^2 = I$ implies $e^{-i\theta P/2} = \cos(\theta/2)I - i\sin(\theta/2)P$ (Euler's formula for Hermitian operators).

**Step 2:** Define $A = G^\dagger(\theta) O G(\theta)$. Expanding:

$$
A \;=\; \left[\cos(\theta/2) I + i\sin(\theta/2) P\right] O \left[\cos(\theta/2) I - i\sin(\theta/2) P\right]
$$

**Step 3:** Distribute:

$$
A \;=\; \cos^2(\theta/2)\, O - i\cos(\theta/2)\sin(\theta/2)\, OP + i\sin(\theta/2)\cos(\theta/2)\, PO + \sin^2(\theta/2)\, POP
$$

**Step 4:** Combine terms. Let $O_0 = (O + POP)/2$ and $O_1 = (O - POP)/(2i)$. Then:

$$
A \;=\; O_0 + \sin(\theta)\, O_1
$$

where the $P$-commutator terms cancel for the $O_0$ part and the $P$-anticommutator terms cancel for $O_1$. (Note: $POP$ involves the same operator conjugacy structure used in the spectral decomposition.)

**Step 5:** Take the expectation value:

$$
\langle O \rangle_\theta \;=\; \langle 0 \vert A \vert 0 \rangle \;=\; c + s \sin(\theta)
$$

where $c = \langle 0 \vert O_0 \vert 0 \rangle$ and $s = \langle 0 \vert O_1 \vert 0 \rangle$ are parameter-independent constants.

**Step 6:** Differentiate:

$$
\frac{\partial \langle O \rangle_\theta}{\partial \theta} \;=\; s \cos(\theta)
$$

**Step 7:** Evaluate at $\theta + \pi/2$ and $\theta - \pi/2$:

$$
\langle O \rangle_{\theta + \pi/2} - \langle O \rangle_{\theta - \pi/2} \;=\; \left[c + s\sin(\theta + \pi/2)\right] - \left[c + s\sin(\theta - \pi/2)\right]
$$

$$
=\; s\cos(\theta) - (-s\cos(\theta)) \;=\; 2s\cos(\theta)
$$

**Step 8:** Equate to the derivative:

$$
\frac{\partial \langle O \rangle_\theta}{\partial \theta} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\left[ \langle O \rangle_{\theta + \pi/2} - \langle O \rangle_{\theta - \pi/2} \right]
$$

**Conclusion:** The gradient can be computed *exactly* by running the circuit twice — once with the parameter shifted by $+\pi/2$ and once with $-\pi/2$ — and taking half the difference. No numerical differentiation is required.

*(7 marks: 2 for statement, 4 for proof, 1 for conclusion)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the absolute value squared in the quantum kernel:** Students often write $K = \langle \psi(\mathbf{x}') \vert \psi(\mathbf{x}) \rangle$ instead of $\vert \langle \psi(\mathbf{x}') \vert \psi(\mathbf{x}) \rangle \vert^2$. Always square the modulus to get a real-valued, positive semi-definite kernel.
> 2. **Confusing the parameter-shift rule with finite differences:** The rule is *exact*; finite differences are *approximate*. Examiners specifically test whether you know the rule produces an exact gradient.
> 3. **Dropping the factor of $1/2$:** Forgetting the $1/2$ coefficient in the parameter-shift rule is a 1-mark deduction. Always include it.
> 4. **Writing $R_y(\theta)$ matrix incorrectly:** The standard form is $R_y(\theta) = e^{-i\theta Y/2} = \begin{pmatrix} \cos(\theta/2) & -\sin(\theta/2) \\ \sin(\theta/2) & \cos(\theta/2) \end{pmatrix}$. Off-by-one sign errors in this matrix lead to wrong final answers in derivation questions.
> 5. **Mixing up angle encoding vs. amplitude encoding:** Angle encoding uses $n$ qubits for $n$ features; amplitude encoding uses $\log_2 n$ qubits for $n$ features. Examiners love testing this distinction.
> 6. **Not labeling the Mermaid diagram node IDs properly:** If you draw a diagram in the exam, never use reserved words like `end` or `subgraph` as node names — KTU has started deducting marks for ambiguous diagrams.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **QML** = Quantum Computing + Classical ML; uses quantum subroutines to accelerate algebraic bottlenecks.
- [x] **QDL** = Deep QML with stacked PQCs acting as quantum analogs of DNN layers.
- [x] **Hilbert space** for $n$ qubits: dimension = $2^n$.
- [x] **Three stages of a VQC**: Feature Encoding → Parameterized Ansatz → Measurement.
- [x] **Feature map** $\mathcal{U}_\phi(\mathbf{x})$: maps classical data $\mathbf{x}$ to quantum state.
- [x] **Quantum kernel** $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = \vert \langle \psi(\mathbf{x}) \vert \psi(\mathbf{x}') \rangle \vert^2$: feeds into classical SVM.
- [x] **Ansatz** $U(\boldsymbol{\theta})$: trainable unitary with rotation + entangling layers.
- [x] **Parameter-shift rule** (must memorize): $\partial_\theta \langle O \rangle = \frac{1}{2}[\langle O \rangle_{\theta+\pi/2} - \langle O \rangle_{\theta-\pi/2}]$.
- [x] **Single-qubit $R_y$ on $\vert 0 \rangle$** gives $\langle Z \rangle = \cos(\theta)$ — the canonical non-linearity of QML.
- [x] **Universal Approximation Theorem** holds for PQCs with sufficient depth and entanglement.
- [x] **QML paradigms**: CC, CQ, QC, QQ — with CQ (variational) being the dominant NISQ-era approach.
- [x] **QCNN, QGAN, Q-Autoencoders** are the three flagship QDL architectures.
- [x] **Gradient cost in PQC**: 2 circuit evaluations per parameter (vs. 1 backward pass classically).
- [x] **Optimizers used in QML**: COBYLA, SPSA, Adam (hybrid), L-BFGS.
- [x] **Barren plateau problem**: gradients vanish exponentially with qubit count — a major challenge.
- [x] **Fidelity** $F(\rho, \sigma) = (\text{Tr}\sqrt{\sqrt{\rho}\sigma\sqrt{\rho}})^2 \in [0,1]$ measures state similarity.
- [x] **Hardware frameworks**: Qiskit (IBM), PennyLane (Xanadu), Cirq (Google), Braket (AWS).
- [x] **CNOT gate**: $\text{CNOT}\vert a, b \rangle = \vert a, a \oplus b \rangle$ — the basic entangler.
- [x] **Encoding types**: Basis, Amplitude, Angle, IQP — know the qubit-vs-feature trade-off for each.
- [x] **Cost functions**: MSE (regression), Cross-Entropy (classification), Hamiltonian expectation (VQE).
- [x] **NISQ era**: Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum — 50–1000 qubits, no error correction, hybrid algorithms.
- [x] **Quantum advantage in QML** is *theoretical* and *task-specific* — not yet demonstrated at scale.
- [x] **Exam weightage**: Expect 7-mark derivations on $\langle Z \rangle = \cos(\theta)$ and 7-mark proofs of parameter-shift rule.

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Future Trends and Challenges in Quantum Computing** refers to the systematic study of the emerging trajectories, anticipated breakthroughs, and the fundamental physical, engineering, and algorithmic obstacles that govern the evolution of quantum information processors beyond the current Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum (NISQ) era. This domain encompasses the architectural roadmaps toward fault-tolerant universal quantum computers, the development of quantum networking/internet, post-quantum cryptographic migration, and the engineering problems of scaling qubits while preserving quantum coherence.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this topic is positioned as the **strategic outlook module** that integrates hardware, software, and theoretical frontiers into a coherent engineering roadmap.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Core Definition Anchors**
> The examiner expects the student to be fluent in the following five conceptual anchors:
> 1. **NISQ Era Limits** — Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum devices (50–1000 qubits, no error correction).
> 2. **Fault-Tolerant Quantum Computing (FTQC)** — logical qubits built from thousands of physical qubits.
> 3. **Quantum Supremacy / Quantum Advantage** — demonstrable computational supremacy over classical supercomputers.
> 4. **Quantum Networking** — entanglement distribution and the Quantum Internet.
> 5. **Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC)** — classical cryptographic hardening against Shor's algorithm.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Quantum Computing Journey"

Imagine quantum computing as a journey akin to **the evolution of classical computing from the ENIAC vacuum-tube era (1945) to the modern smartphone**:

| Era | Classical Computing | Quantum Computing |
|---|---|---|
| **1945–1955** | ENIAC, room-sized, tubes burn out hourly | Current NISQ era (IBM 433-qubit Osprey, Google Sycamore) |
| **1955–1970** | Transistors, integrated circuits | Early FTQC prototypes (logical qubit demos) |
| **1970–2000** | Microprocessors, personal computers | Mid-scale fault-tolerant machines (~10³ logical qubits) |
| **2000–Today** | Cloud, mobile, AI | Quantum cloud platforms (IBM Quantum, AWS Braket) |
| **Future** | Neuromorphic, quantum-classical hybrid | Distributed Quantum Internet |

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Intuition:** Today's quantum computers are roughly where classical computers were in the **late 1940s** — impressive in principle, fragile in practice, and requiring entire rooms of infrastructure. The "challenges" we study are precisely the engineering problems that took classical computing ~30 years to solve.

### 1.3 Key Physical & Engineering Metrics (Standard Quantities)

- **Qubit Coherence Times:** **T₁ ≈ 100 µs (superconducting)** to **T₁ ≈ 10 s (nuclear spin)**.
- **Two-Qubit Gate Error:** Current benchmark **≈ 10⁻³**; fault-tolerance threshold **≈ 10⁻²**.
- **Surface Code Overhead:** **≈ 1,000–10,000 physical qubits per logical qubit**.
- **Quantum Volume (IBM metric):** Doubled roughly every year; current record **QV > 512**.
- **Shor's Algorithm Threat:** Breaks **RSA-2048** in polynomial time on a sufficiently large fault-tolerant machine.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Future Quantum Computing (Decomposed)

#### Pillar 1 — Hardware Scaling Beyond NISQ
- **Superconducting (IBM, Google):** Roadmap toward **100,000+ physical qubits** by ~2030 via 3D integration and cryogenic CMOS control.
- **Trapped Ion (IonQ, Quantinuum):** All-to-all connectivity, **T₁ ~ minutes**, but slower gate times (~µs).
- **Photonic (PsiQuantum, Xanadu):** Room-temperature operation, native networking, but probabilistic gates.
- **Neutral Atom (QuEra, Pasqal):** Scalable arrays using optical tweezers; recent logical-qubit breakthroughs (2023–2024).
- **Topological (Microsoft):** Majorana-based, theoretically protected against decoherence, but unproven experimentally.

#### Pillar 2 — Quantum Error Correction (QEC)
The dominant scaling challenge. Without QEC, any useful algorithm would drown in noise.

#### Pillar 3 — Quantum Software & Compilers
- Higher-level abstractions: **OpenQASM 3.0**, **QIR (Quantum Intermediate Representation)**, **Cirq**, **Qiskit 1.0**.
- **Quantum-classical hybrid workflows** (VQE, QAOA) bridging NISQ to FTQC.

#### Pillar 4 — Quantum Networking & The Quantum Internet
- Entanglement distribution via **quantum repeaters**.
- **E91 protocol** for device-independent QKD.
- Synergies with **classical SDN/SDQ** networks.

#### Pillar 5 — Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC)
- NIST PQC standardization (2024): **CRYSTALS-Kyber**, **CRYSTALS-Dilithium**, **SPHINCS+**.
- Migration of TLS, SSH, blockchain to lattice/hash-based schemes.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory KTU Equations** — These are the high-yield formulas for this topic. Master them.

| # | Formula | LaTeX Form | Meaning / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Coherence decay | $T_2^* \le 2 T_1$ | Dephasing bounded by relaxation (seconds) |
| 2 | Gate fidelity lower bound | $F \ge 1 - \epsilon$ | $\epsilon$ = average gate error (dimensionless) |
| 3 | Threshold theorem | $p < p_{th}$ | Physical error rate must be below threshold (~10⁻² for surface code) |
| 4 | Surface code logical error | $P_L \propto (p/p_{th})^{(d+1)/2}$ | $d$ = code distance, exponential suppression |
| 5 | Physical qubits per logical | $N_{phys} \approx 2 d^2$ | Surface-code footprint (qubits) |
| 6 | Quantum Volume | $QV = \min\bigl[2^n,\, 2^{(1/2)(n+m-n \cdot m/2)}\bigr]$ | $n,m$ = width/depth; dimensionless metric |
| 7 | Shor's complexity | $O\bigl((\log N)^3\bigr)$ | Factoring $N$-bit integer (bit-ops) |
| 8 | Grover's complexity | $O(\sqrt{N})$ | Unstructured search (queries) |
| 9 | QKD key rate (BB84) | $R \approx q \bigl[1 - 2 e\bigr]$ | $e$ = QBER; bits/second |
| 10 | Photon loss (fiber) | $\eta = e^{-\alpha L}$ | $\alpha \approx 0.2$ dB/km telecom fiber |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility — Why these formulas matter in industry:**
> - Formula 4 is the **business case** for fault-tolerant quantum computing. If you can show a customer that **doubling the code distance reduces logical errors quadratically**, you justify a 4× hardware investment.
> - Formula 6 (Quantum Volume) is **IBM's marketing-grade benchmark** — its growth curve is the single most cited metric in investor pitches.
> - Formula 7 is the **existential threat** that triggered NIST's PQC standardization.

### 2.3 Real-World Applications Tied to These Formulas

- **Drug Discovery (Roche, Pfizer):** VQE for molecular ground-state energies — relies on Formulas 1, 2, 8.
- **Cryptography Migration (Google Chrome, Apple iMessage):** PQC rollout driven by Formula 7.
- **Financial Portfolio Optimization (JPMorgan):** QAOA on NISQ hardware — relies on Formulas 5, 6.
- **Quantum Key Distribution (Banking, Govt.):** Already deployed in **Beijing-Shanghai backbone**, **DARPA QPN** — Formula 9.
