## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — Surface Code Logical Error Suppression

We derive the celebrated surface-code scaling law given in **Formula 4** of the cheat sheet.

**Starting Premise:** A distance-$d$ surface code encodes **1 logical qubit** into a 2D grid of $d^2$ data qubits, with stabilizer measurements performed on neighboring ancilla qubits. A single physical error can flip any data qubit; a *logical* error requires an **uncorrectable chain of $d$ errors** spanning the lattice.

Let $p$ = physical error rate per gate/measurement, and $p_{th}$ = threshold (typically $\sim 10^{-2}$ for the surface code).

**Step 1 — Probability of an error chain of length $d$:**
The minimum number of failures that produces an undetectable logical error is a chain of $d$ adjacent errors.

$$P_{\text{chain}}(d) \;\approx\; \binom{d}{d}\, p^{d}\, (1-p)^{d^2 - d}$$

**Explanation:** We choose $d$ specific locations out of $\approx d^2$ to fail. Since $d \ll d^2$, we approximate $(1-p)^{d^2-d} \approx 1$ when $p$ is small.

**Step 2 — Introduce the threshold $p_{th}$:**
Define $p_{th}$ as the critical rate below which the decoder can correct *any* error pattern. The decoder failure probability is dominated by the smallest uncorrectable chain.

$$P_L \;\approx\; c \cdot \left(\frac{p}{p_{th}}\right)^{\frac{d+1}{2}}$$

where $c$ is a constant $\sim O(1)$ capturing the combinatorics of the lattice.

**Step 3 — Justification of the $(d+1)/2$ exponent:**
A code of distance $d$ requires a minimum-weight logical operator of length $d$. The decoder succeeds unless *half* the chain plus one extra error occurs (i.e., $d/2$ errors on each side plus one crossing the boundary).

$$P_L \;\propto\; p^{d/2} \cdot p^{1/2} \;=\; p^{(d+1)/2}$$

**Step 4 — Rescale by $p_{th}$:**
The threshold acts as a natural unit. Below threshold, $\bigl(p/p_{th}\bigr) < 1$, and raising it to the power $(d+1)/2$ gives exponential suppression in $d$.

$$\boxed{\;P_L \;\approx\; c\, \left(\frac{p}{p_{th}}\right)^{\frac{d+1}{2}}\;}$$

**Numerical sanity check:** For $p = 10^{-3}$, $p_{th} = 10^{-2}$, $d = 11$:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_L &\;\approx\; 0.1^{(11+1)/2} \\
    &\;=\; 0.1^{6} \\
    &\;=\; 10^{-6}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **standard KTU numerical**: a 121-qubit surface code reduces a $10^{-3}$ physical error rate to a $10^{-6}$ logical error rate.

### 3.2 Derivation B — Physical-to-Logical Qubit Overhead

For a distance-$d$ rotated planar surface code, the number of physical qubits per logical qubit is:

**Step 1 — Data qubits:** A $d \times d$ grid gives $d^2$ data qubits.

$$N_{\text{data}} = d^2$$

**Step 2 — Ancilla (syndrome) qubits:** Approximately equal to $d^2$ for a 2D layout.

$$N_{\text{ancilla}} \approx d^2$$

**Step 3 — Total:**

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{phys}} &\;=\; N_{\text{data}} + N_{\text{ancilla}} \\
                &\;\approx\; 2 d^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Worked Example — $d = 25$:**

$$N_{\text{phys}} \approx 2 \times 25^2 = 1250 \text{ physical qubits per logical qubit}$$

This is the headline number cited by **Google's 2024 logical qubit roadmap**.

### 3.3 Derivation C — Quantum Volume Growth Bound

IBM's Quantum Volume metric $QV$ is defined as the largest square circuit ($n$ qubits, depth $m = n$) that a device executes with heavy-output probability $> 2/3$.

**Step 1 — Worst-case circuit dimension:** A random $n$-qubit, depth-$n$ circuit acts on a Hilbert space of dimension $2^n$.

$$D_{\text{space}} = 2^n$$

**Step 2 — Ideal success probability** scales as $\sim 1/D_{\text{space}}$; the $2/3$ threshold requires noise to be small enough to preserve distinguishability.

$$QV \le 2^n$$

**Step 3 — The IBM-defined $QV$ is thus:**

$$QV = 2^n \quad \text{where } n \text{ is the largest passing width}$$

**Step 4 — Exponential growth condition:** Doubling $QV$ per year implies

$$QV(t) \approx 2^{(t - t_0)}$$

IBM's published curve: $QV = 4$ (2017) → $QV = 512$ (2022) → projected $QV \approx 10^6$ by 2030.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. Define the NISQ era. Mention any two of its key limitations.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The **NISQ era** (Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum era), a term coined by John Preskill (2018), refers to the current generation of quantum processors with **50 to 1000 physical qubits** that lack full quantum error correction.

**Two key limitations:**
1. **High gate error rates** (~10⁻³), well above the fault-tolerance threshold for deep circuits.
2. **Limited qubit coherence** (T₁, T₂ ~ µs for superconducting), restricting circuit depth.
3. (Optional third) Lack of inter-qubit connectivity / no native multi-qubit logic at scale.

---

**Q2. List any three post-quantum cryptographic algorithms standardized by NIST in 2024.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The NIST Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) standards finalized in **FIPS 203, 204, 205 (August 2024)** are:
1. **CRYSTALS-Kyber** (ML-KEM) — for key encapsulation (lattice-based).
2. **CRYSTALS-Dilithium** (ML-DSA) — for digital signatures (lattice-based).
3. **SPHINCS+** (SLH-DSA) — for digital signatures (hash-based, stateless).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall — KTU Examiner's Warning:**
> Do not write "Shor-resistant" alone as an answer — name the *specific algorithm* and its *category* (lattice / hash / code / multivariate). One mark per correctly-named algorithm + category.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A** *(14 Marks)*

**Q.A (a) — Discuss the major challenges in scaling quantum hardware from the NISQ era to fault-tolerant quantum computing. Include the role of the threshold theorem.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the challenge:** Scaling requires moving from ~1000 noisy physical qubits to ~10⁶ physical qubits with logical-error rates < 10⁻¹⁵.

**Step 2 — Hardware bottlenecks** (write at least three):
- **Decoherence:** Environmental coupling limits T₁, T₂; superconducting qubits struggle beyond ~100 µs.
- **Gate fidelity:** Two-qubit gate errors (~10⁻³) are still above FTQC threshold without correction.
- **Connectivity:** Sparse qubit graphs restrict algorithm compilation; all-to-all is rare outside trapped ions.
- **Control electronics:** Each qubit needs dedicated microwave pulses; cryogenic I/O is a major scaling bottleneck.
- **3D integration & wiring:** Crossing the 10⁴-qubit barrier demands new fabrication paradigms.

**Step 3 — Threshold theorem (Preskill / Aharonov–Ben-Or):**
If physical error rate $p < p_{th}$, arbitrarily long quantum computation is possible with polylogarithmic overhead.

$$\text{Threshold condition:} \quad p < p_{th} \approx 10^{-2}$$

**Step 4 — Surface code implication:** Distance-$d$ code suppresses logical error as
$P_L \propto (p/p_{th})^{(d+1)/2}$.

**Step 5 — Concluding statement:** Hardware scaling is not just a qubit-count problem — it is fundamentally an **error-correction engineering problem**. The threshold theorem guarantees feasibility in principle; engineering must deliver it in practice.

**Q.A (b) — With the help of a suitable block diagram, describe the architecture of a future fault-tolerant quantum computing stack.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Introduction:** A fault-tolerant stack has five functional layers (from bottom to top):
1. Physical qubit layer
2. Control & measurement electronics
3. Quantum error correction (QEC) layer
4. Compiler & runtime layer
5. Application layer

**Step 2 — Layer-by-layer description (each ~1 mark):**

| Layer | Function |
|---|---|
| **Physical** | Superconducting transmons / trapped ions / neutral atoms holding quantum states |
| **Control** | Microwave pulses, lasers, FPGA-based real-time controllers, cryogenic CMOS |
| **QEC** | Surface code, color code, decoder (MWPM, Union-Find) |
| **Compiler** | Qiskit / Cirq / QIR / OpenQASM 3.0 |
| **Application** | Shor, Grover, VQE, QAOA |

**Step 3 — Block diagram (reproduce the figure from Section 4.2 above).** *Award 1 mark for a clean diagram showing all 5 layers.*

**Step 4 — Closing remark on cryogenic/quantum-classical interface:** The interface is itself a frontier — **NVIDIA CUDA-Q**, **IBM Heron**, and **Google Sycamore successors** all push toward tighter integration of classical control with quantum hardware.

---

#### **Question B** *(14 Marks — Alternative)*

**Q.B (a) — Explain Quantum Volume (QV). Compute the QV for an IBM device with $n = 9$ qubits and depth $m = 9$.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition:**
Quantum Volume (Cross et al., 2019) is a single-number benchmark capturing the largest random square circuit a quantum computer can execute successfully.

**Step 2 — Formal definition:**
A device of width $n$ and depth $m$ has QV:
$$QV = 2^{\min(n,m)} \quad \text{if heavy-output probability} > 2/3$$

For square circuits ($n = m$):
$$QV = 2^n$$

**Step 3 — Computation:** For $n = m = 9$:
$$QV = 2^9 = 512$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:** $QV = 512$ corresponds to the **IBM Montreal (2022)** record. It means the device can execute a random 9-qubit, depth-9 circuit with output fidelity above the $2/3$ threshold.

**Step 5 — Significance:** $QV$ doubles roughly yearly (IBM data 2017–2024). It is the *industry's* benchmark, though Google has argued $QV$ underestimates silicon progress.

---

**Q.B (b) — Describe the architecture of the future Quantum Internet. List any two challenges faced in its implementation.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition:**
The Quantum Internet is a global network enabling **entanglement distribution** between geographically separated quantum processors, supporting applications like QKD, blind quantum computing, and distributed quantum sensing.

**Step 2 — Four-layer architecture** (use the diagram from Section 4.3 above):
1. **Application Layer** — Quantum cloud, QKD-secured messaging, distributed sensing.
2. **Transport Layer** — Entanglement distribution protocols (E91, BBM92).
3. **Network Layer** — Quantum repeaters using entanglement swapping.
4. **Physical Layer** — Telecom fiber, free-space links, satellite (China's Micius, 2017).

**Step 3 — Two major challenges:**
1. **Photon loss in fiber:** $\eta = e^{-\alpha L}$ with $\alpha \approx 0.2$ dB/km; classical amplification is forbidden by the no-cloning theorem → **requires quantum repeaters**.
2. **Entanglement swapping fidelity:** Imperfect Bell-state measurements limit cascade length; **deterministic entanglement generation** at each node is an open engineering problem.
3. (Optional 3rd) **Time synchronization** across nodes to within coherence windows.
4. (Optional 4th) **Standardization** — no equivalent of TCP/IP for quantum yet.

**Step 5 — Conclude with milestones:** China's 2000-km Beijing-Shanghai QKD backbone (2017), DARPA QPN, EU Quantum Internet Alliance — all early real-world deployments.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing physical vs. logical qubits.** A 100-qubit NISQ chip is **not** equivalent to 100 logical qubits; the surface-code overhead (~1000×) means you need ~100,000 physical qubits for ~100 logical. Failing to clarify this loses 1–2 marks consistently.
> 2. **Writing "quantum supremacy" instead of "quantum advantage".** Quantum supremacy refers to a specific experimental milestone (e.g., Google's 2019 random-circuit sampling). Quantum advantage is the broader engineering goal. Examiners *will* deduct a mark.
> 3. **Forgetting units in formula sheets.** Always write $T_1$ in **seconds**, error rates as **dimensionless ratios**, QKD key rate in **bits/second**. Unmarked quantities → 0.5–1 mark penalty.
> 4. **Omitting diagrams in long answers.** A 7-mark question in KTU typically allocates **1 mark explicitly for a diagram**. Even a hand-drawn block diagram (clear boxes + arrows) is sufficient.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⭐ **NISQ = 50–1000 noisy qubits; FTQC = 10⁴–10⁶ physical qubits → tens–hundreds of logical qubits.**
- ⭐ **Threshold theorem guarantees fault-tolerance IF physical error $p < p_{th} \approx 10^{-2}$.**
- ⭐ **Surface code overhead: $N_{phys} \approx 2 d^2$. For $d = 25$, that's ~1,250 physical qubits per logical qubit.**
- ⭐ **Logical error scales as $P_L \propto (p/p_{th})^{(d+1)/2}$ — exponential suppression in distance.**
- ⭐ **Quantum Volume doubled yearly: 4 (2017) → 512 (2022) → projected $10^6$ (2030).**
- ⭐ **Shor's algorithm threatens RSA/ECC; NIST PQC standards (Kyber, Dilithium, SPHINCS+) finalized 2024.**
- ⭐ **Five hardware platforms compete:** Superconducting, Trapped Ion, Photonic, Neutral Atom, Topological.
- ⭐ **Five-layer stack:** Physical → Control → QEC → Compiler → Application.
- ⭐ **Quantum Internet needs quantum repeaters** (no-cloning forbids classical amplification).
- ⭐ **Key benchmarks to memorize:** T₁/T₂, gate fidelity, surface-code distance, QV, PQC algorithm names.
